title: Greetings and Farewells
description: Basic greetings and farewells for daily interactions
theory:
👋 Greetings & Farewells: The Social Foundation

Greetings depend on "Social Register." Use formal phrases like "Good morning" (until 12 PM) or "Good afternoon" for strangers and bosses. For friends, use casual ones like "What's up?". Remember: "Good night" is only for leaving or going to bed, never for arriving. Respond to "How are you?" with a brief "I'm well, thanks" to keep conversations smooth and professional.

🎨 Visual & Engaging Examples
  • 📝 Good morning, everyone! → 🌅 [A teacher arriving at school].
  • 📝 Hey! What's up? → ☕ [Meeting a close friend at a café].
  • 📝 It is a pleasure to meet you. → 🤝 [Shaking hands at a job interview].
  • 📝 Goodnight, see you tomorrow. → 🌙 [Leaving the office late at night].
  • 📝 Take care! → 👋 [A friendly goodbye at the end of a call].
🌎 English in the Real World

Scenario A: The Job Interview

  • Candidate: "Good morning, Mr. Smith. It is a pleasure to meet you."
  • Interviewer: "Good morning. Please, have a seat."

Scenario B: The Airport Security

  • Officer: "Good afternoon, sir. Passport, please."
  • Traveler: "Here it is. Good afternoon."

Scenario C: A Casual Party

  • Friend 1: "Hey, Sarah! What's up?"
  • Friend 2: "Not much, just enjoying the music. How's it going?"

title: Personal Introduction
description: How to introduce yourself and ask about others
theory:
🆔 Personal Introduction: Building Your Identity

Introductions follow the "Subject + Verb To Be + Information" formula. A vital rule: in English, you are your age (I am 25), you don't "have" it. Use "I am from" for locations and "I am" for nationalities. Use formal "My name is" for business and casual "I'm" for friends. These simple structures ensure you introduce yourself accurately in any situation.

🎨 Visual & Engaging Examples
  • 📝 My name is Alexander Great. → 🆔 [Presenting an ID card].
  • 📝 I am 25 years old. → 🎂 [Celebrating a birthday].
  • 📝 I'm from Tokyo, Japan. → 🌏 [Pointing to a map].
  • 📝 This is my friend, Maria. → 👈 [Introducing a colleague].
  • 📝 I am a software engineer. → 💻 [Stating a profession].
🌎 English in the Real World

Scenario A: First Day of Class

  • Student: "Hi everyone! I'm Carlos, I'm from Spain, and I'm a student here."
  • Teacher: "Welcome, Carlos! Nice to meet you."

Scenario B: A Networking Event

  • Professional: "Hello, my name is Linda. I am the marketing manager for LevelUp."
  • Guest: "It's a pleasure to meet you, Linda. I'm Mark."

Scenario C: Meeting a Neighbor

  • Neighbor: "Hi! I'm your new neighbor, Jessica."
  • You: "Oh, hi Jessica! I am David. Welcome to the building."

title: The Alphabet
theory:
Mastering the English Alphabet

The English alphabet is the foundation of literacy. Beyond simple letters, mastering the pronunciation and phonetic spelling is crucial for professional communication.

Letter IPA Letter IPA
A /eɪ/ B /biː/
C /siː/ D /diː/
E /iː/ F /ɛf/
G /dʒiː/ H /eɪtʃ/
I /aɪ/ J /dʒeɪ/
K /keɪ/ L /ɛl/
M /ɛm/ N /ɛn/
O /oʊ/ P /piː/
Q /kjuː/ R /ɑːr/
S /ɛs/ T /tiː/
U /juː/ V /viː/
W /ˈdʌbəl.juː/ X /ɛks/
Y /waɪ/ Z /zɛd/ (UK) /ziː/ (US)
🚀 Professional Spelling (Symbols)

In business English, you must know how to spell symbols for email addresses:

  • @ (At)
  • . (Dot)
  • - (Hyphen)
  • _ (Underscore)

title: Numbers
description: Cardinal and ordinal numbers up to 10,000
theory:
🔢 Numbers 1-100

Numbers are essential for prices, ages, dates, and phone numbers. Mastering their pronunciation prevents costly miscommunications.

🚀 The Suffix Secret: -TEEN vs -TY

Many students confuse 13 with 30, or 14 with 40. Here is the key:

Suffix Rule Example
-TEEN (13-19) Long and stressed at the end. Thir-TEEN, Four-TEEN
-TY (20-90) Short, sharp, and unstressed. THIR-ty, FOR-ty
📝 Building Complex Numbers

For numbers greater than 20, simply join the tens and units with a hyphen:

  • 25 → Twenty-five
  • 42 → Forty-two
  • 88 → Eighty-eight

title: Subject Pronouns
description: I, you, he, she, it, we, they
theory:
👤 Subject Pronouns: The Doers

Subject pronouns (I, You, He, She, It, We, They) replace specific names to avoid repetition. Remember that "I" is always capitalized, and "You" serves for both singular and plural. Use "It" for all non-human subjects like objects, animals, or weather. These small words are the foundation of every English sentence, acting as the "doers" of actions.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject Pronoun] + [Verb] + [Complement]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I am a teacher. → 👨‍🏫 [Point to yourself].
  • 📝 She is my boss. → 👩‍💼 [Pointing to a woman].
  • 📝 It is very cold. → ❄️ [Talking about weather].
  • 📝 They are happy. → 👨‍👩‍👧 [Looking at a family].
  • 📝 We are students. → 👨‍🎓👩‍🎓 [A group of people].
  • 📝 You are very kind. → 😊 [Talking to one person].
  • 📝 He is my brother. → 👦 [Male relative].
  • 📝 You are all welcome. → 👐 [Talking to many people].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Introducing a group: "We are here for the meeting."

2. Talking about a pet: "This is my dog. It is very friendly."

3. Describing a colleague: "John is busy. He is in a call."

title: Verb "To Be"
description: Am, is, are — usage and forms
theory:
✅ Verb To Be: States & Identity

The verb "To Be" (Am, Is, Are) is the most important verb in English. It doesn't describe actions, but rather states, identities, and locations. Use "Am" only with "I," "Is" for singular (He/She/It), and "Are" for plural (You/We/They). Mastering this allows you to define the world around you with precision.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [am / is / are] + [Noun/Adj]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I am happy. → 😊 [Current state].
  • 📝 She is a doctor. → 👩‍⚕️ [Identity].
  • 📝 We are in London. → 🇬🇧 [Location].
  • 📝 It is 10:00 AM. → ⏰ [Time].
  • 📝 They are tired. → 😴 [Physical state].
  • 📝 You are my friend. → 🤝 [Relationship].
  • 📝 The water is cold. → ❄️ [Quality].
  • 📝 He is 20 years old. → 🎂 [Age].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Professional Introductions: "I am the CEO of this company."

2. Describing feelings: "I am excited about the project today!"

3. Identifying objects: "That is my laptop on the table."

title: Wh- Questions
description: Who, what, where, when, why, how
theory:
❓ Wh- Questions: Seeking Info

Wh- words (Who, What, Where, When, Why, How) are used to request specific information rather than a simple "Yes/No." In English, the Wh- word always starts the sentence. "Where" asks for place, "When" for time, and "Why" for reason. These are your primary tools for investigation and learning in any new environment.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Wh- Word] + [Verb] + [Subject] + ?

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Who is he? → 👨 [Person].
  • 📝 What is this? → 📦 [Object].
  • 📝 Where are you? → 📍 [Location].
  • 📝 When is lunch? → 🥪 [Time].
  • 📝 Why are you sad? → 😢 [Reason].
  • 📝 How are you? → 👋 [Manner/State].
  • 📝 Which do you want? → 🔴🔵 [Choice].
  • 📝 How much is it? → 💰 [Price].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Traveling: "Where is the train station, please?"

2. Networking: "What do you do for a living?"

3. Scheduling: "When does the meeting start?"

title: Demonstratives
theory:
📦 Demonstratives: Pointing Out

Demonstratives (This, That, These, Those) tell us where an object is relative to the speaker. Use "This/These" for things near you, and "That/Those" for things far away. "This/That" are singular, while "These/Those" are plural. This spatial grammar is essential for shopping, giving directions, and identifying objects in a shared space.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Demonstrative] + [Noun] + [Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 This apple. → 🍎 [Near/Singular].
  • 📝 That car. → 🚗... [Far/Singular].
  • 📝 These books. → 📚📚 [Near/Plural].
  • 📝 Those stars. → ✨✨ [Far/Plural].
  • 📝 This is my phone. → 📱 [Holding it].
  • 📝 That is my house. → 🏠 [Pointing away].
  • 📝 Do you like these? → 👟👟 [Near].
  • 📝 Look at those! → 🦅🦅 [Far].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Shopping: "How much is this shirt? No, not that one, this one here."

2. Pointing at a menu: "I would like those tacos, please."

3. Introducing people: "Mom, this is my friend, Alex."

title: Possessives
description: My, your, his, her, its, our, their
theory:
👥 Possessives: Ownership

Possessive adjectives (My, Your, His, Her, Its, Our, Their) show who "owns" or is related to a noun. Unlike some languages, they do not change based on the object (plural/singular), only based on the owner. "His" is for a male owner, "Her" for female. Correct usage is critical for clarifying relationships and property in any conversation.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Possessive] + [Noun]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 My car. → 🚗 [Belongs to me].
  • 📝 Your keys. → 🔑 [Belongs to you].
  • 📝 His father. → 👨 [His relationship].
  • 📝 Her book. → 📖 [Her property].
  • 📝 Its tail. → 🐕 [Belongs to the dog].
  • 📝 Our team. → ⚽ [Belongs to us].
  • 📝 Their house. → 🏠 [Belongs to them].
  • 📝 My friends. → 👥 [My relationship].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. At the office: "Is this your pen? No, it's his pen."

2. Talking about family: "Our parents are on vacation in Hawaii."

3. Clarifying ownership: "The cat is washing its paws."

title: What Time Is It?
description: Telling and asking for the time
theory:
⏰ What Time Is It?

Telling time in English uses two main systems: the Digital (10:15) and the Traditional (Quarter past ten). Use "At" for specific times (At 5 PM) and "It is" to state the current time. Mastering time is not just about numbers; it's about punctuality, making appointments, and managing your professional schedule effectively.

🧩 Grammar Formula

It is + [Time] + [o'clock / AM / PM]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 It's 5:00. → 🕔 [Five o'clock].
  • 📝 It's half past six. → 🕡 [6:30].
  • 📝 It's quarter to eight. → 🕖 [7:45].
  • 📝 At midnight. → 🌑 [12:00 AM].
  • 📝 It's noon. → ☀️ [12:00 PM].
  • 📝 Ten past four. → 🕓 [4:10].
  • 📝 Twenty to nine. → 🕘 [8:40].
  • 📝 Sharp! → 📍 [Exactly on time].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Setting a meeting: "Can we meet at 3:30 PM tomorrow?"

2. Catching a flight: "The gate closes at quarter past ten."

3. Casual checking: "Excuse me, do you have the time? Yes, it's 2:20."

title: Prepositions of Time
description: In, on, at — using prepositions with time
theory:
📅 Prepositions of Time: In, On, At

Use "At" for precise times (at 8 PM), "On" for days and dates (on Monday), and "In" for longer periods like months, years, and seasons (in June). This "Pyramid Rule" (Specific to General) helps you organize your life and schedule without grammatical confusion.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[at/on/in] + [Time/Date/Period]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 At 9 o'clock. → 🕘 [Specific time].
  • 📝 On Friday. → 🗓️ [Day of the week].
  • 📝 In 2024. → 🎆 [Year].
  • 📝 On July 4th. → 🇺🇸 [Specific date].
  • 📝 At night. → 🌙 [Exception rule].
  • 📝 In the morning. → 🌅 [Part of the day].
  • 📝 On my birthday. → 🎂 [Special day].
  • 📝 In summer. → 🏖️ [Season].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Planning a trip: "We are leaving in August, on a Saturday."

2. Work schedule: "The shift starts at 8:00 AM every day."

3. History: "The company was founded in the 19th century."

title: Simple Present
theory:
🔄 Simple Present: Daily Routines

The Simple Present describes facts, habits, and permanent situations. A crucial rule: for He, She, and It, always add an "-s" to the verb (I work, He works). This tense is your primary tool for describing who you are and what you do every day, providing a stable foundation for all communication.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Verb (+s)] + [Complement]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I drink coffee. → ☕ [Habit].
  • 📝 She works hard. → 💼 [3rd person -s].
  • 📝 Dogs bark. → 🐕 [General fact].
  • 📝 We live in Spain. → 🇪🇸 [Permanent state].
  • 📝 He plays guitar. → 🎸 [Skill/Hobby].
  • 📝 It rains a lot. → 🌧️ [Weather fact].
  • 📝 You speak English. → 🗣️ [Ability].
  • 📝 They study daily. → 📚 [Routine].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Job description: "I manage a team of five people and report to the director."

2. Introducing a city: "Paris attracts millions of tourists every year."

3. Morning routine: "I wake up at 6, take a shower, and have breakfast."

title: Adverbs of Frequency
theory:
📊 Adverbs of Frequency

Adverbs (Always, Often, Sometimes, Never) tell us how often something happens. Rule of Thumb: place them before main verbs but after the verb "To Be." They range from 100% (Always) to 0% (Never). Mastering these allows you to describe your personality and habits with mathematical precision.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Adverb] + [Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I always brush my teeth. → 🪥 [100%].
  • 📝 I usually walk to work. → 🚶‍♂️ [90%].
  • 📝 She is often late. → ⏰ [After verb To Be].
  • 📝 We sometimes eat out. → 🍕 [50%].
  • 📝 He rarely smokes. → 🚬🚫 [10%].
  • 📝 They never lie. → 😇 [0%].
  • 📝 It hardly ever snows. → ❄️🚫 [Very rare].
  • 📝 You frequently call. → 📞 [Often].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Health checkup: "How often do you exercise? I usually go twice a week."

2. Performance review: "He always completes his tasks on time."

3. Social habits: "We never work on Sundays; we prefer to rest."

title: Comparatives
theory:
⚖️ Comparatives: Better & Faster

Use comparatives to describe differences between two objects. For short words, add "-er" (Fast → Faster). For long words, use "More" (Expensive → More expensive). Always use "Than" to introduce the second item. This is essential for making decisions, shopping, and expressing preferences between two options.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Item A] + [be] + [Comparative] + than + [Item B]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 A plane is faster than a car. → ✈️ > 🚗.
  • 📝 This is more expensive. → 💰💰💰.
  • 📝 I am taller than you. → 🦒.
  • 📝 He is better than me. → ⭐ Irregular!
  • 📝 It's colder today. → ❄️.
  • 📝 Worse than before. → 👎 Irregular!
  • 📝 Smaller than a house. → 🐭.
  • 📝 More difficult. → 🧩.
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Buying a phone: "The Pro model is faster but also more expensive than the Basic."

2. Job interview: "I believe I am more experienced than other candidates."

3. Weather: "Madrid is usually hotter than London in the summer."

title: Food
theory:
🍎 Food: Nutrition & Dining

Food vocabulary is essential for survival and social interaction. Categories include Proteins (meat, fish), Carbohydrates (bread, rice), and Vegetables. Use "Countable" for items you can count (an apple) and "Uncountable" for mass nouns (water, salt). This knowledge is the key to ordering at restaurants and grocery shopping abroad.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Countable]: a / an / numbers | [Uncountable]: some / any

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 An apple. → 🍎 [Countable].
  • 📝 Some water. → 💧 [Uncountable].
  • 📝 A loaf of bread. → 🍞 [Unit of measure].
  • 📝 Fresh vegetables. → 🥦🥕 [Plural].
  • 📝 I'm hungry. → 🤤 [State].
  • 📝 This is delicious! → 😋 [Adjective].
  • 📝 I'm allergic to nuts. → 🥜🚫 [Health info].
  • 📝 Spicy food. → 🌶️ [Flavor].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Ordering: "I'll have the chicken with some rice, please."

2. Shopping: "Do we have any eggs left in the fridge?"

3. Social: "What's your favorite type of food? I love Italian cuisine."

title: Likes and Dislikes
theory:
❤️ Likes and Dislikes

To express preferences, use verbs like Love, Like, Don't mind, Dislike, and Hate. Rule: after these verbs, the next verb must be in the -ing form (gerund). This allows you to share your personality, hobbies, and interests with others in a natural and engaging way.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Verb of preference] + [Verb-ing / Noun]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I love dancing. → 💃 [High preference].
  • 📝 She likes pizza. → 🍕 [Positive].
  • 📝 We don't mind waiting. → ⏳ [Neutral].
  • 📝 He hates getting up early. → 😴🚫 [Strong negative].
  • 📝 They dislike cold weather. → ❄️ [Negative].
  • 📝 Do you like singing? → 🎤 [Question].
  • 📝 I quite like reading. → 📖 [Moderate positive].
  • 📝 I can't stand traffic. → 🚗🚗 [Intense dislike].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Making friends: "I really like playing video games. What about you?"

2. At a restaurant: "I don't like spicy food, so I'll take the salad."

3. Job interview: "I enjoy working in a team and solving problems."

title: Present Continuous
theory:
📸 Present Continuous: Happening NOW

Use the Present Continuous (Am/Is/Are + Verb-ing) for actions happening at the moment of speaking. It's like a live photo of your life. It is also used for temporary situations that are true right now but not permanent. This tense brings "movement" to your English.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [be] + [Verb-ing] + [Complement]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I am working. → 💻 [Right now].
  • 📝 She is eating. → 🍱 [Action in progress].
  • 📝 They are playing. → ⚽ [Currently].
  • 📝 Are you listening? → 👂 [Question].
  • 📝 It is raining. → 🌧️ [Weather now].
  • 📝 We are studying. → 📚 [Current activity].
  • 📝 He isn't sleeping. → 👀 [Negative progress].
  • 📝 Is she coming? → 🚶‍♀️ [Upcoming now].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. On the phone: "I can't talk now, I am driving."

2. Describing a scene: "Look! The cat is chasing the bird!"

3. Temporary work: "I am staying in London for two weeks for a project."

title: Simple vs. Continuous
description: When to use simple vs. continuous tenses
theory:
⚖️ Simple vs. Continuous

The Simple Present is for routines and facts (I always work). The Present Continuous is for actions happening right now (I am working today). Use time markers like "Usually" for Simple and "At the moment" for Continuous to distinguish between your permanent life and your current activity.

🧩 Grammar Formula

Habit: Subject + Verb | Now: Subject + be + Verb-ing

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I eat breakfast daily. → 🥣 [Routine].
  • 📝 I am eating right now. → 🍱 [Current].
  • 📝 She lives in NY. → 🏙️ [Permanent].
  • 📝 She is visiting Paris. → 🗼 [Temporary].
  • 📝 Does he work? → 💼 [General].
  • 📝 Is he working? → 💻 [Right now].
  • 📝 It rains a lot here. → 🌧️ [Fact].
  • 📝 It is raining today. → ☔ [Current].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Professional life: "I usually work from home, but today I am attending a conference."

2. Describing habits: "He speaks four languages, and currently he is learning Chinese."

3. Social status: "We don't play football often, but we are playing a match this afternoon."

title: Simple Past
theory:
🎞️ Simple Past: The Finished Path

The Simple Past describes actions that were completed at a specific time in the past. Regular verbs end in "-ed" (worked), while Irregular verbs change completely (go → went). Use the auxiliary "Did" for questions and negatives (didn't work). This is the primary tense for storytelling and reporting history.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [V2 / Verb-ed] + [Complement]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I worked yesterday. → 💼 [Regular].
  • 📝 I went to the park. → 🌳 [Irregular].
  • 📝 She saw a movie. → 🎬 [Finished].
  • 📝 They didn't call. → 📞🚫 [Negative].
  • 📝 Did you eat? → 🍱? [Question].
  • 📝 We bought a car. → 🚗 [Completed].
  • 📝 It was cold. → ❄️ [Past state].
  • 📝 He finished his tea. → ☕ [Action over].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Recapping your day: "I woke up, had breakfast, and walked to the office."

2. Reporting an event: "The project started in June and ended in December."

3. Historical facts: "The company opened its first store in 1995."

title: Describing Places
description: Vocabulary and phrases for describing locations
theory:
🏘️ Describing Places

To describe a place, use "There is / There are" and prepositions of place (In, On, Under, Next to). Use adjectives to define quality (modern, crowded, quiet, beautiful). This vocabulary allows you to act as a guide, find your way in a city, and describe your home or office accurately.

🧩 Grammar Formula

There is/are + [Noun] + [Preposition] + [Place]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 A crowded street. → 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦🚶‍♂️ [Adjective].
  • 📝 There is a park. → 🌳 [Existence].
  • 📝 It is next to the bank. → 🏦 [Preposition].
  • 📝 A quiet library. → 🤫📚 [Adjective].
  • 📝 There are many shops. → 🛍️🛍️ [Plural].
  • 📝 The city is modern. → 🏙️ [Quality].
  • 📝 In front of the car. → 🚗 [Position].
  • 📝 A beautiful view. → 🌅 [Opinion].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Booking a hotel: "Is there a gym in the hotel? Yes, it's on the top floor."

2. Tourist info: "The museum is quiet in the mornings but crowded at noon."

3. Relocation: "My new flat is very modern and it's near the center."

title: Giving Directions
description: Finding your way and helping others
theory:
🗺️ Giving Directions

Giving directions requires Imperatives (Go, Turn, Stop) and sequence markers (First, Then, After that). Common phrases include "Go straight ahead," "Turn left/right," and "Go past the..." Being able to give and follow directions is a critical survival skill for any traveler or expat.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Imperative Verb] + [Preposition/Direction]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Go straight. → ⬆️ [Movement].
  • 📝 Turn left. → ⬅️ [Change direction].
  • 📝 Go past the park. → 🌳🏃‍♂️ [Continue].
  • 📝 On the corner. → 📐 [Location].
  • 📝 Take the second right. → 🔀 [Specific turn].
  • 📝 At the end of the road. → 🏁 [Final point].
  • 📝 Across from the cafe. → ☕↔️ [Opposite].
  • 📝 Between the bank and the shop. → 🏦🏠 [Middle].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. In the street: "Excuse me, how do I get to the station? Go straight and turn right at the light."

2. Helping a tourist: "The museum is next to the park. You can't miss it!"

3. GPS guidance: "In 200 meters, turn left onto Main Street."

title: Superlatives
theory:
🏆 Superlatives: The Absolute Best

Use superlatives (The best, The biggest, The most beautiful) to compare one object against a whole group. For short words, add "-est" (Fastest). For long words, use "The most." Always include the word "The." This allows you to highlight extremes and state unique facts about the world.

🧩 Grammar Formula

the + [Short Adj + -est] | the most + [Long Adj]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The tallest building. → 🏙️ [Extreme size].
  • 📝 The best food. → ⭐ Irregular (Good → Best).
  • 📝 The most expensive. → 💎 [High cost].
  • 📝 The shortest day. → ⏳ [Minimum duration].
  • 📝 The worst movie. → 👎 Irregular (Bad → Worst).
  • 📝 The hottest desert. → 🏜️ [Temperature extreme].
  • 📝 The most interesting. → 🧠 [Subjective extreme].
  • 📝 The easiest way. → ✅ [Simplicity].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Booking a trip: "This is the cheapest flight available today."

2. Giving reviews: "That was the most delicious meal I have ever eaten!"

3. Trivia: "Mount Everest is the highest mountain in the world."

title: Present Perfect
description: Life experiences with have/has + past participle
theory:
🔄 Present Perfect: Experience

The Present Perfect (Have/Has + Past Participle) connects the past to the present. It is used for experiences where the exact time is not important. "I have visited Rome" means it happened at some point in your life. This is the tense for sharing life stories and professional accomplishments.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [have / has] + [V3 / Participle]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I have traveled. → ✈️ [Life experience].
  • 📝 She has eaten. → 🍱 [Action done].
  • 📝 Have you seen it? → 👀 [Question].
  • 📝 They haven't finished. → ⏳ [Ongoing].
  • 📝 We have lived here. → 🏠 [Status].
  • 📝 He has lost his keys. → 🔑🚫 [Past action with present result].
  • 📝 Has she called? → 📞? [Past event inquiry].
  • 📝 It has stopped raining. → 🌦️ [Recent change].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Interviewing: "I have worked with many international teams in the past."

2. Traveling: "I have been to Japan three times, it's amazing."

3. Progress reports: "We have already reached our goals for this month."

title: Present Perfect vs. Past
description: When to use present perfect vs simple past
theory:
⚖️ Present Perfect vs. Past Simple

The Past Simple is for specific, finished times (Yesterday I ate). The Present Perfect is for indefinite times or experiences (I have eaten). Use the Past Simple to give details and the Present Perfect to announce an action or share a general experience.

🧩 Grammar Formula

Finished Time: Past Simple | Unfinished/Experience: Present Perfect

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I went to Paris in 2010. → 🇫🇷 [Specific year].
  • 📝 I have been to Paris. → 🗼 [General experience].
  • 📝 She lost her keys 1h ago. → 🔑🚫 [Specific time].
  • 📝 She has lost her keys. → 🔍 [Present result].
  • 📝 We lived there in 1990. → 🏚️ [Over].
  • 📝 We have lived here for 5 years. → 🏠 [Continuing].
  • 📝 Did you see him? → 👀 [Context].
  • 📝 Have you seen him? → 🤔 [Ever].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Social interaction: "I have seen that movie. I watched it last night with my brother."

2. Breaking news: "The company has expanded! They opened five new stores last week."

3. Daily life: "I have already done the dishes. I finished them ten minutes ago."

title: Already, Yet, Since & For
description: Time adverbs used with present perfect
theory:
⏱️ Already, Yet, Since & For

These time markers refine the Present Perfect. "Already" (before expected), "Yet" (not until now), "Since" (specific start time), and "For" (duration of time). Using these accurately shows a high level of English proficiency, allowing you to manage timelines and expectations effectively.

🧩 Grammar Formula

since + [Start Date] | for + [Duration of time]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I've already done it. → ✅ [Done].
  • 📝 Not yet. → ⏳ [Waiting].
  • 📝 Since 2010. → 📍 [Point in time].
  • 📝 For ten years. → 📏 [Length of time].
  • 📝 I've just eaten. → 🍱 [Seconds ago].
  • 📝 Since last night. → 🌙 [Specific start].
  • 📝 For two hours. → 🕓 [Duration].
  • 📝 I've never been there. → 🚫 [Zero frequency].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Work history: "I have worked at this company for five years, since 2019."

2. Checking status: "Have you finished the report yet? Yes, I've already sent it."

3. Relationships: "We have known each other for a long time, since we were at university."

title: Describing People
theory:
👤 Describing People

To describe people, use the verb "To Be" for qualities (She is tall) and the verb "To Have" for physical features (She has blue eyes). Use adjectives for personality (friendly, smart) and appearance (beautiful, slim). This is essential for introductions, reporting missing people, or describing your friends to others.

🧩 Grammar Formula

be + [Adj (Tall/Smart)] | have + [Noun (Hair/Eyes)]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 She is tall. → 🦒 [Height].
  • 📝 He has blue eyes. → 👁️👁️ [Feature].
  • 📝 They are friendly. → 😊 [Personality].
  • 📝 Short hair. → 💇‍♂️ [Appearance].
  • 📝 Smart student. → 🧠 [Quality].
  • 📝 Middle-aged man. → 🧔 [Age].
  • 📝 Wavy hair. → 〰️ [Style].
  • 📝 Funny person. → 😂 [Personality].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Introducing a friend: "This is Sarah. She is very kind and she has long blonde hair."

2. Job application: "I am looking for a candidate who is hardworking and reliable."

3. Describing a suspect: "He was tall and he had a small beard."

title: Modal Verbs
description: Can, could, should, must and their uses
theory:
🛠️ Modal Verbs: Ability & Permission

Modal verbs like Can and Could describe ability (Can → present, Could → past) and permission. Rule: modals never change their form (no "-s") and are followed by a base verb. These are the tools that "modify" verbs to show what is possible or allowed in your world.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [can / could / should] + [Base Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I can swim. → 🏊‍♂️ [Present ability].
  • 📝 I could run fast. → 🏃‍♂️ [Past ability].
  • 📝 Can I go? → 🚪 [Permission].
  • 📝 You should rest. → 🛌 [Advice].
  • 📝 Could you help? → 🤝 [Polite request].
  • 📝 I can't hear. → 👂🚫 [Negative ability].
  • 📝 We can play. → ⚽ [Possibility].
  • 📝 She can sing. → 🎤 [Skill].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Interview: "I can use Excel and I can speak two languages."

2. Giving advice: "You should see a doctor if you feel sick."

3. Politeness: "Could you repeat that, please? I didn't hear you."

title: Future
description: Will and Be going to for future plans
theory:
🚀 Future with "Going to"

Use Be Going To for future plans and intentions that you have already decided. It is also used for predictions based on current evidence (e.g., look at those clouds!). This tense allows you to project your life forward and share your goals and certainties with others.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [am/is/are] + going to + [Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I'm going to travel. → ✈️ [Plan].
  • 📝 She's going to cook. → 🍳 [Intention].
  • 📝 They are going to win. → 🏆 [Prediction].
  • 📝 Are you going to call? → 📞? [Plan inquiry].
  • 📝 It's going to rain. → 🌧️ [Evidence-based].
  • 📝 We are going to study. → 📚 [Decision made].
  • 📝 He isn't going to come. → 🚫 [Negative plan].
  • 📝 Is she going to work? → 💼? [Question].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Planning a holiday: "I am going to visit Spain next summer; I already booked the flight."

2. Weather warning: "Look at the sky! It is going to be a storm."

3. Business meeting: "Next quarter, we are going to launch three new products."

title: Giving Messages
theory:
📧 Giving Messages

To give messages, use reporting verbs like Say and Tell. Rule: use "Say" for general statements (He says that...) and "Tell" when you specify the listener (He tells me...). This skill is vital for acting as a link between people and reporting information accurately in office or social settings.

🧩 Grammar Formula

say + [something] | tell + [someone] + [something]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Tell him I'm late. → 🏃‍♂️ [Message].
  • 📝 Say hello. → 👋 [General].
  • 📝 Tell me the truth. → 💡 [Recipient included].
  • 📝 He said he's tired. → 😴 [Reporting].
  • 📝 She told me her name. → 🆔 [Specific person].
  • 📝 Can you tell her? → 🤔 [Request].
  • 📝 He says no. → 🚫 [Direct report].
  • 📝 Tell them to wait. → ⏳ [Instruction].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Office task: "Can you tell the boss that I'm finished with the report?"

2. Social relay: "John said he can't come tonight; he's feeling sick."

3. Customer service: "Please tell the client that their order is ready for pickup."

title: Talking About Changes
description: How things have changed over time
theory:
🔄 Talking About Changes

To describe changes, use the Present Perfect (has changed) or comparative adjectives (it's getting better). Use verbs like Become, Grow, and Get to show transformation. This vocabulary is the bridge to describing progress, evolution, and the passage of time in your own life and the world.

🧩 Grammar Formula

get / become + [Comparative Adjective]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 It's getting better. → 📈 [Progress].
  • 📝 She became a doctor. → 👩‍⚕️ [Transformation].
  • 📝 The city has changed. → 🏗️ [Evolution].
  • 📝 I'm getting old. → 👴 [Natural change].
  • 📝 He grew up fast. → 👦→🧔 [Maturity].
  • 📝 My English is improving. → 🗣️⭐ [Positive change].
  • 📝 It's getting dark. → 🌑 [Environmental change].
  • 📝 Life has become easy. → 😊 [Result].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Personal growth: "I feel like my confidence is getting stronger every day."

2. Technology: "Phones have become much smaller and faster in the last decade."

3. Economic news: "Prices are getting higher because of the inflation."

title: There was / There were
description: Past existence of things and people
theory:
⏳ There was / There were

Use "There was" (singular) and "There were" (plural) to describe the existence of things or people in the past. It is the past form of "There is / There are." This structure is essential for describing scenes, reporting missing items, or explaining what was available at an event yesterday.

🧩 Grammar Formula

There was + [Singular Noun]

There were + [Plural Noun]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 There was a dog. → 🐕 [One animal].
  • 📝 There were many people. → 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 [Group].
  • 📝 There wasn't any milk. → 🥛🚫 [Uncountable/Singular negative].
  • 📝 Were there any cookies? → 🍪🍪? [Question].
  • 📝 There was a party. → 🥳 [Event].
  • 📝 There were two cars. → 🚗🚗 [Plural].
  • 📝 Was there a problem? → ⚠️ [Singular question].
  • 📝 There weren't any seats. → 🪑🚫 [Plural negative].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Reporting a theft: "There was a laptop on my desk, but now it's gone!"

2. Describing a trip: "There were so many beautiful flowers in the park yesterday."

3. Checking availability: "Was there enough food for everyone at the meeting?"

title: Used to + infinitive
description: Past habits that are no longer true
theory:
🕰️ Used to + Infinitive

"Used to" describes habits or states that were true in the past but are no longer true. It emphasizes a change in lifestyle or routine. In negatives and questions, remember to remove the "d" (didn't use to). This is your best tool for reminiscing and comparing your past self with who you are today.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + used to + [Base Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I used to smoke. → 🚬🚫 [I don't now].
  • 📝 She used to have long hair. → 💇‍♀️ [It's short now].
  • 📝 We didn't use to like sushi. → 🍣 [We like it now].
  • 📝 Did you use to live here? → 🏠 [Questioning the past].
  • 📝 He used to play piano. → 🎹 [Past hobby].
  • 📝 There used to be a cinema here. → 🏗️ [The building changed].
  • 📝 I used to study every night. → 📖 [Old routine].
  • 📝 They didn't use to travel. → ✈️ [New habit].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Childhood memories: "I used to play with LEGOs for hours when I was ten."

2. Career changes: "He used to work as a waiter, but now he is a manager."

3. City evolution: "This neighborhood used to be very quiet ten years ago."

title: Past Simple vs. Past Continuous
theory:
🎬 Past Simple vs. Past Continuous

This "Cinematic" grammar uses the Past Continuous for background actions (was/were -ing) and the Past Simple for the main events that interrupt them. "While" usually precedes the continuous action, and "When" precedes the interruption. This allows you to tell stories that feel alive and well-structured.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Progress action] + when + [Interrupting action]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I was sleeping when the phone rang. → 😴 🔔 [Interruption].
  • 📝 While she was cooking, she cut her finger. → 🍳 🔪 [Accident].
  • 📝 They were dancing when the music stopped. → 💃 🔇 [Finished].
  • 📝 It was raining when we arrived. → 🌧️ 📍 [Background].
  • 📝 I saw him while I was walking. → 👀 🚶‍♂️ [Observation].
  • 📝 We were talking when he entered. → 🗣️ 🚪 [Sudden event].
  • 📝 The cat jumped while I was reading. → 🐈 📖 [Background].
  • 📝 Were you working when I called? → 💻 📞 [Question].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Explaining a mistake: "I was sending an email when the internet went down."

2. Reporting an incident: "The car was driving fast when it hit the tree."

3. Describing a scene: "The sun was shining and people were laughing when the news arrived."

playing"}]}]}

title: Comparisons and Evaluations
theory:
⚖️ Comparisons and Evaluations

Beyond simple "er/more," we use (not) as... as for equality, and Too/Enough for evaluations. "Too" means "more than necessary" (too hot), while "Enough" means "sufficient" (hot enough). These tools are essential for expressing opinions, giving feedback, and making choices based on quality and quantity.

🧩 Grammar Formula

as + [Adj] + as | too + [Adj] | [Adj] + enough

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 He is as tall as me. → 🦒=🦒 [Equality].
  • 📝 This soup is too hot. → 🥵 [Negative evaluation].
  • 📝 I'm strong enough. → 💪 [Positive evaluation].
  • 📝 It isn't as good as the old one. → 👎 [Inferiority].
  • 📝 The shoes are too small. → 👟🚫 [Problem].
  • 📝 Is it big enough? → 📦? [Questioning capacity].
  • 📝 She runs as fast as a tiger. → 🐅 [Comparison].
  • 📝 You are too kind! → 😊 [Extreme quality].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Reviewing a product: "This laptop is as fast as the Pro version but too expensive."

2. Job requirements: "He is experienced enough for the role, but not as fast as Sarah."

3. Practical life: "I don't have enough money for a new car; this one is old enough anyway."

title: Wish + Past Tense
theory:
🌟 Wish + Past Tense: Imaginary Present

Use "Wish" followed by the Past Simple to express regrets or desires about the present (things you want to change now). Although we use a past verb, the meaning is current. Rule: use "Were" for all persons (I wish I were). This is your primary way to express dreams, goals, and dissatisfaction with current reality.

🧩 Grammar Formula

I wish + [Subject] + [Past Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I wish I had a car. → 🚗🚫 [I don't have one now].
  • 📝 I wish I were rich. → 💰 [I'm not rich].
  • 📝 She wishes she could fly. → 🦅 [Impossible dream].
  • 📝 I wish it stopped raining. → 🌧️ [Current desire].
  • 📝 They wish they lived in Paris. → 🗼 [Location desire].
  • 📝 I wish I knew the answer. → 🧠 [Lack of info].
  • 📝 He wishes he were taller. → 🦒 [Physical regret].
  • 📝 I wish you were here. → 🤝 [Distance regret].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Work frustration: "I wish I didn't have to work today; the weather is beautiful."

2. Travel dreams: "I wish we spoke Japanese so we could visit Tokyo easily."

3. Social life: "I wish I had more free time to see my friends."

title: Indirect Questions
description: Polite ways to ask for information
theory:
🗣️ Indirect Questions: Polite Enquiries

Indirect questions are more polite than direct ones. Use phrases like "Could you tell me..." or "Do you know...". Crucial Rule: the word order returns to a statement order (Subject + Verb) and we do not use "do/does/did." This is the key to professional and respectful communication in English.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Polite Phrase] + Wh-word + [Subject] + [Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Could you tell me where the bank is? → 🏦 [Polite location check].
  • 📝 Do you know what time it is? → ⏰ [Time check].
  • 📝 I wonder if she is coming. → 🤔 [Yes/No indirect].
  • 📝 Can you explain how this works? → ⚙️ [Instruction check].
  • 📝 Do you have any idea why he left? → 🚪 [Reason check].
  • 📝 I'd like to know when it starts. → 🗓️ [Schedule check].
  • 📝 Could you say who called? → 📞 [Identify check].
  • 📝 I'm not sure if they are ready. → ⏳ [Status check].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Customer Service: "Could you tell me how much this costs? I don't see the price."

2. Asking for directions: "Excuse me, do you know where the nearest station is?"

3. Business meeting: "I'd like to know what the main goals are for this quarter."

title: Adverbs of Time
theory:
🗓️ Adverbs of Time

Adverbs like Already, Yet, Still, and Just tell us when an action happens relative to now. "Already" (before expected), "Yet" (not until now - in negatives/questions), and "Still" (continuing longer than expected). These small words are essential for tracking progress and managing expectations in time-sensitive situations.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Adverb] + [Verb] | [Subject] + [Verb] + yet

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I have already eaten. → 🍱 [Done early].
  • 📝 He hasn't called yet. → 📞🚫 [Waiting].
  • 📝 I am still working. → 💻 [Continuing].
  • 📝 I just arrived. → 📍 [Seconds ago].
  • 📝 Have you finished yet? → 🤔 [Question].
  • 📝 She already knows. → 🧠 [No need to tell her].
  • 📝 We still need help. → 🆘 [Continuing need].
  • 📝 Recently, it rained. → 🌧️ [A short time ago].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Project management: "I've already sent the file, but he hasn't replied yet."

2. Daily routine: "Are you still in bed? It's 10 AM!"

3. Travel news: "The train has just left the station; you missed it by a minute."

title: Future: be going to vs. will
theory:
🚀 Future: be going to vs. will

Use Be Going To for plans, intentions, and predictions based on evidence. Use Will for spontaneous decisions, promises, and predictions based on opinion. Choosing between them reveals your level of commitment and the evidence you have for your future claims.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[be] going to + [Verb] | will + [Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I'm going to travel. → ✈️ [Plan/Tickets bought].
  • 📝 I will help you! → 🤝 [Spontaneous offer].
  • 📝 Look at those clouds! It's going to rain. → ☁️ [Evidence].
  • 📝 I think it will rain. → 🤔 [Opinion].
  • 📝 I will marry you. → 💍 [Promise].
  • 📝 We are going to meet at 5. → 🗓️ [Prior arrangement].
  • 📝 I won't tell anyone. → 🤐 [Negative promise].
  • 📝 They are going to win. → 🏆 [Score is 5-0].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Weekend plans: "I'm going to visit my parents this weekend; I already told them."

2. Restaurant order: "I'll have the steak, please." (Spontaneous decision → Will).

3. Weather prediction: "It's going to be a long winter; look at how much snow we have already."

title: Modal Verbs for Necessity
description: Must, have to, and their negatives
theory:
🛡️ Modal Verbs for Necessity

Modal verbs like Must, Have to, and Need to express different levels of obligation. "Must" is for personal feelings or rules, "Have to" for external obligations (laws/bosses), and "Need to" for essential requirements. Mastering these helps you navigate rules and expectations in a foreign country or workplace.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [must / have to / need to] + [Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I must study. → 📚 [Internal desire].
  • 📝 You have to wear a seatbelt. → 🚗 [Law/External].
  • 📝 I need to drink water. → 💧 [Essential requirement].
  • 📝 He mustn't smoke here. → 🚭 [Prohibition].
  • 📝 We don't have to pay. → 🆓 [No obligation].
  • 📝 She needs to leave soon. → 🏃‍♀️ [Necessary action].
  • 📝 You must be quiet. → 🤫 [Strong rule].
  • 📝 Do I have to go? → 🤔 [Questioning necessity].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Airport rules: "You must show your passport to board the plane."

2. Workplace: "I have to finish this report by 5:00 PM today."

3. General health: "You need to sleep at least 8 hours to stay healthy."

title: Modal Verbs for Requests
theory:
🙏 Modal Verbs for Requests

Use modals like Can, Could, May, and Would to ask for things politely. "Can" is informal, while "Could" and "May" are more formal. "Would you mind...?" is the highest level of politeness. This is your social "lubricant" for asking favors and making requests without sounding rude.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Modal] + [Subject] + [Verb] + [Complement] + ?

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Can I have a coffee? → ☕ [Informal].
  • 📝 Could you help me? → 🤝 [Polite].
  • 📝 May I come in? → 🚪 [Formal permission].
  • 📝 Would you mind opening the door? → 🪟 [Very polite].
  • 📝 Would you like some tea? → 🍵 [Offering].
  • 📝 Can you pass the salt? → 🧂 [Informal request].
  • 📝 May we leave now? → 🏃‍♀️ [Seeking permission].
  • 📝 Could I borrow your pen? → ✍️ [Polite request].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. In a shop: "Could you show me that jacket in blue, please?"

2. With friends: "Can you give me a ride home tonight?"

3. Professional setting: "Would you mind sending me the minutes of the meeting?"

(Would you please...)", "a": "Would you please help me?"}]}]}

title: Phrasal Verbs - Separable and Non-Separable
theory:
🧩 Phrasal Verbs: Combinations

Phrasal verbs are combinations of a verb + a particle (e.g., Get up, Turn off). Some are Separable (you can put the object in the middle: turn the TV off) and others are Inseparable (you cannot separate them: look for keys). These are the hallmark of natural, native-like English fluency.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Verb] + [Preposition/Particle]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Wake up! → ⏰ [Stop sleeping].
  • 📝 Turn off the light. → 💡🚫 [Separable].
  • 📝 Look for my keys. → 🔍 [Inseparable].
  • 📝 Get along with him. → 🤝 [Relationship].
  • 📝 Pick up the phone. → 📞 [Separable].
  • 📝 Carry on working. → 💻 [Continue].
  • 📝 Give up smoking. → 🚬🚫 [Stop a habit].
  • 📝 Find out the truth. → 💡 [Discover].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Social interaction: "I get along really well with my new coworkers."

2. Solving a problem: "We need to find out why the server crashed."

3. Daily routine: "Please turn off the computer when you're done."

title: Quantifiers
description: Some, any, much, many, a lot of
theory:
📊 Quantifiers: How much?

Quantifiers (Much, Many, A lot of, A few, A little) tell us the amount of something. Rule: use "Many/A few" for Countable items (books) and "Much/A little" for Uncountable mass (water). "A lot of" works for both! This precision is key for business inventory, recipes, and financial discussions.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Quantifier] + [Noun]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Many people. → 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 [Countable plural].
  • 📝 Much sugar. → 🧂 [Uncountable].
  • 📝 A few coins. → 🪙🪙 [Small countable amount].
  • 📝 A little time. → ⏳ [Small uncountable amount].
  • 📝 A lot of energy. → ⚡ [Large amount - both].
  • 📝 Too many emails. → 📧📧📧 [Negative excess].
  • 📝 Not enough water. → 💧🚫 [Insufficient].
  • 📝 Plenty of food. → 🍗🍕 [More than enough].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Cooking: "Add a little salt and a few drops of oil."

2. In the office: "There are too many tasks for only a few people."

3. Shopping: "We have plenty of time, don't worry."

title: Relative Clauses (who, which, that)
description: Connecting and describing with relative pronouns
theory:
🔗 Relative Clauses: Connecting Ideas

Relative clauses (who, which, that) help us define nouns without starting a new sentence. Use "Who" for people, "Which" for things/animals, and "That" for both in casual English. This "glue" allows you to create complex, detailed descriptions and professional-sounding narratives.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Noun] + [who / which / that] + [Clause]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The man who is tall. → 👨🦒 [Defining person].
  • 📝 The car which is red. → 🚗🔴 [Defining object].
  • 📝 The book that I read. → 📖 [Defining object].
  • 📝 The person who called me. → 📞 [Subject clause].
  • 📝 The city where I live. → 🏙️ [Location clause].
  • 📝 The day when we met. → 🗓️ [Time clause].
  • 📝 The girl whose bag is blue. → 👜 [Possession clause].
  • 📝 A thing that matters. → ❤️ [Importance].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Identifying people: "The woman who works in HR is very helpful."

2. Specifying objects: "This is the computer that keeps crashing."

3. Narrating: "It was a day when everything went perfectly."

(This is the book...)", "a": "This is the book which won the prize"}, {"q": "I know a place. We can eat there. (I know a place...)", "a": "I know a place where we can eat"}]}]}

title: Gerunds vs. Infinitives
description: When to use -ing vs to + verb
theory:
🎭 Gerunds vs. Infinitives

When two verbs follow each other, the second one is either a Gerund (eating) or an Infinitive (to eat). Rule: "Enjoy/Hate" usually take gerunds, while "Want/Hope" take infinitives. This is one of the most common sources of error for learners, so memorizing the patterns is key to sounding natural.

🧩 Grammar Formula

Verb A + [Verb-ing / to + Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I want to go. → 🚶‍♂️ [Infinitive].
  • 📝 I enjoy reading. → 📖 [Gerund].
  • 📝 She hopes to win. → 🏆 [Future goal].
  • 📝 He hates waiting. → ⏳ [Activity dislike].
  • 📝 We plan to visit. → ✈️ [Intention].
  • 📝 Stop talking. → 🤐 [Ceasing activity].
  • 📝 I forgot to call. → 📞🚫 [Failed obligation].
  • 📝 Keep trying! → 💪 [Continuous effort].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Professional goals: "I hope to be promoted by the end of the year."

2. Leisure: "I love swimming in the ocean during summer."

3. Instructions: "Remember to lock the door when you leave."

title: Zero Conditional
theory:
⚖️ Zero Conditional: Facts

The Zero Conditional (If + Present, Present) describes universal truths, scientific facts, or rules. If the condition happens, the result always happens. It is the grammar of logic, science, and absolute certainties in the world.

🧩 Grammar Formula

If + [Present Simple], + [Present Simple]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 If you heat water, it boils. → 🌡️🔥 [Scientific fact].
  • 📝 If I'm tired, I sleep. → 😴 [Habitual truth].
  • 📝 If it rains, the ground gets wet. → 🌧️ [Logic].
  • 📝 Plants die if they don't get water. → 🌿🚫 [Rule of nature].
  • 📝 If you press the button, it starts. → 🔘 [Instruction].
  • 📝 I get a headache if I don't drink coffee. → ☕🚫 [Physical truth].
  • 📝 If you mix red and blue, you get purple. → 🎨 [Fact].
  • 📝 Ice melts if it is warm. → 🧊💧 [Science].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Cooking instructions: "If you cook it for 20 minutes, it is ready."

2. Workplace rules: "If you arrive late, the door locks automatically."

3. Explaining nature: "If the sun goes down, it gets dark."

title: First Conditional
theory:
🚀 First Conditional: Real Possibilities

The First Conditional (If + Present, Will + Verb) describes real possibilities in the future. It shows the result of a specific condition that is likely to happen. This is essential for planning, negotiating, and predicting results based on current actions.

🧩 Grammar Formula

If + [Present Simple], + will + [Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 If it rains, I will stay home. → 🌧️🏠 [Plan].
  • 📝 If you study, you will pass. → 🎓 [Prediction].
  • 📝 I will call you if I arrive early. → 📞 [Promise].
  • 📝 If we hurry, we won't be late. → 🏃‍♂️ [Possibility].
  • 📝 She will be happy if she gets the job. → 😊💼 [Potential result].
  • 📝 If you don't eat, you will be hungry. → 🤤 [Logic].
  • 📝 They will buy it if the price is good. → 💰 [Condition].
  • 📝 If I see him, I will tell him. → 👀 [Future action].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Negotiating: "If you give me a discount, I will buy two."

2. Warnings: "If you don't finish the report today, the manager will be angry."

3. Promises: "I will help you with your homework if I have time tonight."

title: Dates
theory:
🗓️ Dates (Fechas)

Telling dates in English requires **Ordinal Numbers** (1st, 2nd, 3rd) and specific prepositions. In American English, we usually say "Month Day" (July 4th), while in British English, it's "Day Month" (The 4th of July). Mastering this ensures you never miss an appointment or an important celebration.

🧩 Grammar Formula

on + [Month] + [Ordinal Number]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 January 1st. → 🎆 [First day of the year].
  • 📝 March 3rd. → 🗓️ [Third day].
  • 📝 May 22nd. → 🎂 [Twenty-second].
  • 📝 October 31st. → 🎃 [Thirty-first].
  • 📝 Born in 1995. → 👶 [Year only].
  • 📝 Party on Saturday. → 🥳 [Day only].
  • 📝 Meeting on June 15th. → 💼 [Specific date].
  • 📝 The 2nd of February. → 🇬🇧 [British style].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Filling forms: "Date of birth: September 12th, 1990."

2. Making appointments: "The dentist is available on April 5th at 10 AM."

3. Historical events: "The company was founded on August 1st, 2010."

title: Tag Questions
description: Confirmation questions at the end of statements
theory:
❓ Tag Questions: Confirmation

Tag questions are small questions at the end of a sentence (e.g., ..., isn't it?). They are used to check information or ask for agreement. Rule: if the sentence is Positive, the tag is Negative, and vice versa. This is a key conversational tool for building rapport and confirming facts.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Positive sentence], + [Negative tag]?

[Negative sentence], + [Positive tag]?

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 You are hungry, aren't you? → 🍕? [Expecting 'yes'].
  • 📝 He isn't here, is he? → 🚫? [Expecting 'no'].
  • 📝 It's cold, isn't it? → ❄️? [Agreement].
  • 📝 She speaks English, doesn't she? → 🗣️? [Checking].
  • 📝 We won't be late, will we? → 🏃‍♀️? [Reassurance].
  • 📝 They like music, don't they? → 🎵? [Agreement].
  • 📝 You can swim, can't you? → 🏊‍♂️? [Ability check].
  • 📝 He was late, wasn't he? → ⏰? [Past confirmation].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Small talk: "Beautiful day, isn't it? Yes, it's lovely!"

2. Checking details: "The meeting is at 2:00 PM, isn't it? No, it's at 3:00."

3. Confirmation: "You've sent the email, haven't you? Oh, I forgot! I'll do it now."

title: Express Agreement (So do I / Neither do I)
description: Agreeing with positive and negative statements
theory:
🤝 Express Agreement: So & Neither

To agree with someone, use "So do I" (for positive sentences) and "Neither do I" (for negative ones). The auxiliary must match the original sentence (So am I, So can I, Neither did I). Mastering this prevents the repetitive "Me too / Me neither" and makes you sound much more advanced.

🧩 Grammar Formula

So + [Auxiliary] + I | Neither + [Auxiliary] + I

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 "I'm hungry." - So am I. → 🤤🤤 [Agreement].
  • 📝 "I don't like tea." - Neither do I. → 🍵🚫 [Agreement].
  • 📝 "I can swim." - So can I. → 🏊‍♂️ [Matching modal].
  • 📝 "I didn't go." - Neither did I. → 🏠 [Matching past].
  • 📝 "I've finished." - So have I. → ✅ [Matching present perfect].
  • 📝 "She is tired." - So am I. → 😴 [Matching state].
  • 📝 "I won't call." - Neither will I. → 📞🚫 [Matching future].
  • 📝 "I love pizza." - So do I. → 🍕 [Matching present].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Socializing: "I love traveling." - "So do I! I went to Japan last year."

2. Complaints: "I can't stand this noise." - "Neither can I, it's terrible."

3. Office talk: "I haven't seen the new manager." - "Neither have I."

(Neither...)", "a": "Neither can I"}, {"q": "I didn't go. (Neither...)", "a": "Neither did I"}]}]}

title: Stative Verbs
description: Verbs that describe states, not actions
theory:
🧠 Stative Verbs: States of Being

Stative verbs (Know, Love, Believe, Want, Hate) describe states rather than physical actions. Crucial Rule: these verbs are rarely used in the continuous (-ing) form. You say "I know," never "I am knowing." Understanding this prevents one of the most identifiable mistakes in English grammar.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Stative Verb] (Present Simple only)

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I believe you. → 💡 [Mental state].
  • 📝 She loves chocolate. → 🍫 [Emotion].
  • 📝 This belongs to me. → 📦 [Possession].
  • 📝 It costs $10. → 💰 [Measurement].
  • 📝 I want some water. → 💧 [Desire].
  • 📝 Do you understand? → 🧠 [Cognition].
  • 📝 This smells good. → 👃 [Sense].
  • 📝 He seems nice. → 😊 [Appearance].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Feelings: "I need a break right now." (NOT "I am needing").

2. Opinions: "I think that's a great idea."

3. Senses: "This music sounds very loud to me."

title: Passive Voice (Simple Forms)
description: Focus on the action, not the doer
theory:
⚙️ Passive Voice: Focus on Action

The Passive Voice (Be + Past Participle) is used when the action is more important than who did it, or when the person is unknown. "The window was broken" focuses on the window, not the person. This is essential for professional reporting, scientific writing, and news stories.

🧩 Grammar Formula

Object + [be] + [Past Participle]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The cake was eaten. → 🍰🚫 [Unknown eater].
  • 📝 Phones are made in China. → 📱🇨🇳 [General fact].
  • 📝 The letter was sent. → ✉️ [Action focused].
  • 📝 Rice is grown here. → 🌾 [Agricultural fact].
  • 📝 The pyramids were built long ago. → 🏗️ [History].
  • 📝 Spanish is spoken in Mexico. → 🗣️ [Language fact].
  • 📝 The car was stolen. → 🚗🚫 [Crime report].
  • 📝 These books are read by millions. → 📖 [Statistics].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Product labels: "This item was designed in California and assembled in Vietnam."

2. News reports: "The thief was caught early this morning."

3. Business info: "Over 1,000 emails are sent from this office every day."

title: Indefinite Pronouns
theory:
👤 Indefinite Pronouns: Someone & Everywhere

Indefinite pronouns (Someone, Anything, Nowhere) refer to non-specific people, things, or places. Rule: use "-body/one" for people, "-thing" for objects, and "-where" for locations. Use "Any-" for negatives and questions. This allows you to talk about generalities without needing specific names.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Some / Any / No / Every] + [body / thing / where]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Someone is at the door. → 🚪👤 [Unknown person].
  • 📝 I have nothing to eat. → 🍱🚫 [Zero objects].
  • 📝 Is anywhere open? → 🛍️? [Any place?].
  • 📝 Everyone is happy. → 😊😊😊 [Total group].
  • 📝 Something is wrong. → ⚠️ [Unknown issue].
  • 📝 I looked everywhere. → 🗺️ [All places].
  • 📝 Nobody knows. → 🤐 [Zero people].
  • 📝 Anything is possible. → 🌟 [General potential].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Losing something: "I lost my keys! I've looked everywhere but I can't find anything."

2. Party planning: "Does anyone want more cake? Everyone seems to be finished."

3. Problem solving: "Something is wrong with my car. I need someone to fix it."

title: Too vs. Enough
theory:
⚖️ Too vs. Enough: Evaluation

"Too" means "more than what is needed" (negative excess) and goes before the adjective. "Enough" means "sufficient" and goes after the adjective. This subtle placement is the difference between complaining about a problem and expressing satisfaction with a result.

🧩 Grammar Formula

too + [Adj] | [Adj] + enough

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 It's too hot. → 🥵 [Uncomfortable].
  • 📝 It's warm enough. → 😊 [Comfortable].
  • 📝 Too many people. → 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦... [Crowded].
  • 📝 Not fast enough. → 🐢 [Slow].
  • 📝 Too much noise. → 🔊💥 [Loud].
  • 📝 Big enough. → 📦✅ [Fits].
  • 📝 Too expensive. → 💰💰💰 [Can't buy].
  • 📝 Old enough to vote. → 🗳️ [Qualified].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Restaurant feedback: "The food was good, but the music was too loud for a conversation."

2. Shopping: "This shirt is too small. Do you have a big enough size in blue?"

3. Job skills: "I'm not experienced enough for this role yet; it's too advanced for me."

title: So vs. Such
theory:
🌟 So vs. Such: Emphasis

Use "So" with adjectives (So good!) and "Such" with noun phrases (Such a good book!). Both are used to add extreme emphasis to what you are saying. They often lead to a "That" clause to show a result (It was so cold that we stayed home).

🧩 Grammar Formula

so + [Adjective] | such a(n) + [Adjective] + [Noun]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 So beautiful! → 😍 [Emphasis].
  • 📝 Such a nice day! → ☀️ [Emphasis + Noun].
  • 📝 So tired. → 😴 [State].
  • 📝 Such a big car. → 🚙 [Object].
  • 📝 So many people. → 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 [Quantity].
  • 📝 Such a mess! → 🧹🚫 [Situation].
  • 📝 So expensive. → 💰 [Cost].
  • 📝 Such an interesting story. → 📖 [Noun phrase].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Complimenting: "You are so kind! It was such a pleasure meeting you."

2. Complaining: "The exam was so difficult! I've never seen such a hard test."

3. Describing a trip: "We had such a great time! The weather was so perfect."

title: Both, Either, Neither
theory:
⚖️ Both, Either, Neither

These words are used to talk about two things. "Both" (A and B), "Either" (A or B), and "Neither" (not A and not B). They are essential for presenting choices, finding common ground, and clarifying selections between two options.

🧩 Grammar Formula

both ... and | either ... or | neither ... nor

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I like both. → 🍎🍏 [Two items].
  • 📝 Either red or blue. → 🔴/🔵 [Choice].
  • 📝 Neither of them. → ❌❌ [Zero of two].
  • 📝 Both parents. → 👨👩 [Relationship].
  • 📝 You can have either. → 🎁? [Permission].
  • 📝 Neither cold nor hot. → ⚖️ [Neutral].
  • 📝 Both are correct. → ✅✅ [Verification].
  • 📝 I don't like either. → 👎👎 [Negative choice].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Ordering food: "Do you want tea or coffee? Either is fine for me, thanks."

2. Family: "Both my brothers live in London now."

3. Regrets: "I looked at two cars, but neither of them was in good condition."

title: Adverbs for Possibility
theory:
🤔 Adverbs for Possibility

Adverbs like Maybe, Perhaps, Probably, and Definitely show how sure you are about an action. "Definitely" (100%), "Probably" (75%), "Maybe" (50%). Placing these correctly (usually after "be" or before main verbs) helps you express nuance and manage expectations.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Adverb] + [Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Maybe I will go. → ❓ [Uncertain].
  • 📝 I'll probably stay. → 🏠 [Likely].
  • 📝 I definitely agree. → ✅ [100% sure].
  • 📝 He perhaps forgot. → 🧠🚫 [Possibility].
  • 📝 They possibly left. → 🚪 [Possibility].
  • 📝 It will surely rain. → 🌧️ [Strong belief].
  • 📝 Likely to win. → 🏆 [High chance].
  • 📝 I'm certainly ready. → 💪 [Absolute].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Plans: "Will you come to the party? Maybe, I have a lot of work."

2. Forecasting: "The company will probably expand next year if profits continue."

3. Confirming: "Are you coming tomorrow? Definitely! See you at 8."

title: Modals for Prohibition
theory:
🚫 Modals for Prohibition

To tell someone what is NOT allowed, use Mustn't (strong rule) or Can't (informal rule/ability). Rule: "Mustn't" means it is forbidden, while "Don't have to" simply means it isn't necessary. Mastering this distinction prevents dangerous or socially awkward misunderstandings of rules.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [mustn't / can't] + [Base Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 You mustn't smoke. → 🚭 [Prohibition].
  • 📝 You can't park here. → 🅿️🚫 [Rule].
  • 📝 Don't touch! → 🚫 [Command].
  • 📝 We mustn't be late. → ⏰ [Crucial rule].
  • 📝 You can't enter. → 🚪🚫 [No permission].
  • 📝 Children mustn't play here. → 🚸 [Safety rule].
  • 📝 No running! → 🏃‍♂️🚫 [Short rule].
  • 📝 You may not use it. → 📱🚫 [Formal].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. At a hospital: "You mustn't use your mobile phone near the equipment."

2. Driving: "You can't turn left at this intersection; it's a one-way street."

3. Home rules: "You mustn't eat in the living room; only in the kitchen."

title: Second Conditional
theory:
🌈 Second Conditional: Imaginary Life

The Second Conditional (If + Past, Would + Verb) is used for hypothetical or imaginary situations in the present or future. It describes dreams and things that are unlikely to happen. Rule: use "Were" for all subjects (If I were you). This is the grammar of "What if...?"

🧩 Grammar Formula

If + [Past Simple], + would + [Base Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 If I won the lottery, I would travel. → 🎫💰 [Hypothetical].
  • 📝 If I were you, I would go. → 🤝 [Advice].
  • 📝 She would buy a car if she had money. → 🚗 [Imaginary].
  • 📝 If it snowed in Spain, I would be happy. → ❄️ [Unlikely].
  • 📝 Would you live in Mars if you could? → 🚀 [Dream question].
  • 📝 If he called, I would answer. → 📞 [Possible but unlikely].
  • 📝 We would help if we were there. → 📍 [Distance regret].
  • 📝 I wouldn't do that if I were him. → ❌ [Opinion].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Giving advice: "If I were you, I would talk to the manager about the problem."

2. Dreaming: "If I spoke ten languages, I would work for the UN."

3. Business brainstorming: "What would happen if we doubled our prices?"

title: First vs Second Conditional
theory:
⚖️ First vs. Second Conditional

The First Conditional is for real possibilities (If I have time, I'll go). The Second Conditional is for imaginary dreams (If I had a million dollars, I'd go). Choosing between them shows the listener how likely you think the situation really is.

🧩 Grammar Formula

Real: If + Present, Will | Imaginary: If + Past, Would

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 If I see him, I will tell him. → 🚶‍♂️ [Possible].
  • 📝 If I saw a ghost, I would run. → 👻 [Unlikely].
  • 📝 If it rains, we'll cancel. → 🌧️ [Real threat].
  • 📝 If it snowed in the desert, I'd be shocked. → 🏜️❄️ [Dream].
  • 📝 I'll buy it if it's cheap. → 💰 [Plan].
  • 📝 I'd buy it if it were cheap. → 💰 [But it's expensive].
  • 📝 If he studies, he'll pass. → 📖 [Possible].
  • 📝 If he studied, he'd pass. → 😴 [But he doesn't study].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Planning: "If the weather is nice tomorrow, we will go to the beach." (First Conditional).

2. Dreaming: "If I were a bird, I would fly to Hawaii." (Second Conditional).

3. Negotiating: "If you pay today, I will give you a discount." (First Conditional).

title: Past Modals
description: Should have, could have, would have
theory:
🕰️ Past Modals: Regrets & Deduction

Past modals (Should have, Could have, Might have) are used to talk about the past with a current perspective. "Should have" is for regrets (I should have studied). "Might have" is for past possibilities (I might have lost my keys). These are essential for self-reflection and analyzing past mistakes.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Modal] + have + [V3 / Participle]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I should have stayed. → 🏠 [Regret].
  • 📝 He could have won. → 🏆 [Missed opportunity].
  • 📝 They might have left. → 🚪 [Deduction].
  • 📝 You must have seen it. → 👀 [High certainty].
  • 📝 I would have called. → 📞 [Willingness in past].
  • 📝 Shouldn't have eaten that. → 🤢 [Regret].
  • 📝 She couldn't have known. → 🧠🚫 [Impossibility].
  • 📝 We ought to have checked. → 📋 [Obligation regret].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Regretting a choice: "I should have taken the train; the traffic is terrible."

2. Solving a mystery: "Where are my glasses? I must have left them at the office."

3. Apologizing: "I'm sorry, I could have handled that situation better."

title: Reported Speech (Statements & Requests)
theory:
🗣️ Reported Speech: Statements

Reported speech is used to tell someone what another person said. Rule: "Backshift" the tense (Present → Past). "I'm tired" becomes "He said he was tired." Pronouns also change (I → He/She). This is the key to reporting conversations, news, and workplace updates accurately.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + said / told + [Reported Clause]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 "I like pizza" → He said he liked pizza. → 🍕 [Reporting].
  • 📝 "I'm here" → She said she was there. → 📍 [Backshift].
  • 📝 "We are late" → They said they were late. → ⏰ [Plural report].
  • 📝 "I'll call" → He said he would call. → 📞 [Future report].
  • 📝 "I can go" → She said she could go. → 🚶‍♀️ [Modal report].
  • 📝 "I've finished" → He said he had finished. → ✅ [Perfect report].
  • 📝 She told me her name. → 🆔 [Recipient included].
  • 📝 They said they'd help. → 🤝 [Contraction report].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Reporting a meeting: "The manager said that the project was on schedule."

2. Gossip: "Mary told me that she was moving to London next month."

3. Relaying messages: "He said he would be ten minutes late because of traffic."

title: Reported Speech (Modals)
theory:
🗣️ Reported Speech: Modals & Requests

In reported speech, modals like "Can" become "Could" and "Will" becomes "Would." For requests, use the formula "Tell + someone + to + verb" (e.g., He told me to wait). This specialized grammar is essential for reporting instructions and relaying complex information in professional settings.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + told me to + [Verb]

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 "Can you help?" → He asked if I could help. → 🤝 [Reporting a request].
  • 📝 "Wait here" → She told me to wait there. → ⏳ [Reporting a command].
  • 📝 "Don't smoke" → He told us not to smoke. → 🚭 [Negative command].
  • 📝 "I will help" → She said she would help. → 🤝 [Reporting a promise].
  • 📝 "I can't come" → He said he couldn't come. → 🚫 [Negative ability].
  • 📝 "Open the door" → She asked me to open the door. → 🚪 [Reporting a polite request].
  • 📝 "May I leave?" → He asked if he might leave. → 🚶‍♂️ [Reporting permission].
  • 📝 Would you mind...? → She asked me to... → 🗣️ [High politeness].
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Relaying boss's orders: "The boss told me to finish the report by tomorrow."

2. Reporting a conversation: "I asked him if he could join us for dinner, but he said he couldn't."

3. Instructions: "The doctor told me not to eat sugar for a week."

title: Relative Pronouns
description: Master defining and non-defining relative clauses
theory:
🔗 Relative Pronouns (B1)

Relative pronouns (who, which, that, whose, where) are the "connectors" of English. They allow you to combine two separate thoughts into one sophisticated sentence by adding a relative clause. This is vital at the B1 level to avoid repetitive, simple sentences and to sound more fluent. "Who" identifies people, "which" identifies objects, and "whose" indicates possession.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Main Clause] + [Relative Pronoun] + [Defining Information]

Example: "The man (who) lives next door is nice."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The candidate who we interviewed yesterday was very impressive. → 👨‍💼 [Identifying a specific person].
  • 📝 The office which has the glass walls is the conference room. → 🏢 [Specifying an object/place].
  • 📝 That is the boy whose father works at NASA. → 🚀 [Showing possession/relationship].
  • 📝 The city where I grew up is now a tech hub. → 🏙️ [Indicating a specific location].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Omit (v): To leave out. "You can sometimes omit the pronoun."
  • Redundant (adj): Repetitive. "Avoid redundant words like 'he' after 'who'."
  • Specify (v): To state clearly. "Use 'which' to specify the object."
  • Relationship (n): Connection. "Whose shows a relationship."
  • Clause (n): Part of a sentence. "This is a relative clause."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Professional Introductions: "This is Maria, who will be leading the project."

2. Describing Properties: "We are looking for a house that has at least three bedrooms."

3. Identifying Belongings: "Is this the bag which you lost at the airport?"

title: Gerund Phrases as Subjects
description: Use gerunds to start sentences naturally
theory:
🧠 Gerund Phrases as Subjects (B1)

A gerund is the -ing form of a verb acting as a noun. When you use a gerund phrase as the subject, it allows you to talk about activities, habits, or concepts as singular ideas. This structure is more advanced and natural-sounding than using "To + verb". It is commonly used in academic essays, health advice, and general rules.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Verb + -ing] + [Singular Verb (is/makes)] + [Complement]

Example: "Reading (is) fun."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Learning a new language requires patience and time. → 📖 [Activity as a requirement].
  • 📝 Swimming in the ocean is more difficult than in a pool. → 🌊 [Comparing activities].
  • 📝 Working from home has become very popular lately. → 🏠 [Modern lifestyle trend].
  • 📝 Saving money early in life is a smart decision. → 💰 [Wise advice].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Requirement (n): Something needed. "Learning is a requirement."
  • Abstract (adj): Not physical. "Hiring is an abstract concept."
  • Balanced (adj): Equal parts. "Use gerunds for a balanced sentence."
  • Consistency (n): Staying the same. "Practice requires consistency."
  • Efficiency (n): Working well. "Organizing improves efficiency."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Health Advice: "Exercising daily is the best way to stay healthy."

2. Academic Success: "Taking notes during lectures helps you remember details."

3. Business Rules: "Arriving late to meetings is considered unprofessional."

title: Gerunds and Infinitives
description: Know which verbs take -ing or to + verb
theory:
🔄 Gerunds and Infinitives (B1)

Verbs like 'enjoy', 'avoid', and 'suggest' must be followed by a gerund (-ing), whereas verbs like 'decide', 'hope', and 'plan' must be followed by an infinitive (to + verb). At the B1 level, this is a critical test of your fluency. Mastering these patterns eliminates common "learner errors" and helps you sound much more like a native speaker.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Verb A] + [Gerund (-ing)] OR [Infinitive (to + verb)]

Example: "I enjoy (singing)" vs "I want (to sing)".

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I enjoy listening to podcasts during my commute. → 🎧 [Enjoy + -ing].
  • 📝 We decided to take a taxi instead of the bus. → 🚕 [Decide + to].
  • 📝 She hopes to visit Japan next summer. → 🗼 [Hope + to].
  • 📝 You should avoid eating too much sugar. → 🍭 [Avoid + -ing].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Preference (n): What you like more. "State your preference."
  • Commitment (n): A promise to do. "Making a commitment to study."
  • Hesitate (v): To wait before doing. "Don't hesitate to ask."
  • Intend (v): To plan to do. "I intend to finish today."
  • Suggest (v): To give an idea. "I suggest going now."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Making Plans: "I suggest meeting at 5 PM."

2. Career Goals: "I hope to become a manager one day."

3. Daily Habits: "I avoid checking emails after 8 PM."

title: Describing Problems 1
description: Talk about damage using adjectives and nouns
theory:
🛠️ Describing Problems 1 (B1)

Being able to accurately describe physical problems is essential for professional and daily life. Whether you are reporting a broken laptop to IT or a leaking pipe to a landlord, using specific past participles (like cracked, broken, or stained) ensures your message is clear and the solution can be found quickly.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [is/are] + [Past Participle (Adjective)]

Example: "The screen (is) cracked."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The window is cracked and needs replacement. → 🪟 [Surface damage].
  • 📝 The faucet is leaking under the sink. → 💧 [Fluid escaping].
  • 📝 This shirt is stained with coffee. → ☕ [Mark on fabric].
  • 📝 The machine is malfunctioning again. → ⚙️ [Not working correctly].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Defective (adj): Not working correctly. "The battery is defective."
  • Hazard (n): A danger. "A leaking pipe is a hazard."
  • Inspect (v): To look at closely. "The plumber will inspect it."
  • Repair (v/n): To fix. "The repair was expensive."
  • Restore (v): To bring back to original state. "We restored the desk."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Reporting Issues: "I need to report a cracked screen on my company laptop."

2. Home Maintenance: "The roof is leaking, so we need to call a professional."

3. Customer Service: "I'm returning this item because it's defective."

title: Describing Problems 2
theory:
🛠️ Describing Problems 2 (B1)

Moving beyond physical damage, this lesson focuses on service and abstract problems like delays, miscommunications, and instability. In a globalized world, explaining these nuances is key to managing expectations and resolving conflicts in international teams or during travel.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[There is/was] + [Noun (delay/issue)] + [Context]

Example: "There was (a delay) in the flight."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 There is a delay in the delivery of our supplies. → ⏳ [Time issue].
  • 📝 The system is overloaded with requests. → 💻 [Technical capacity].
  • 📝 There was a misunderstanding about the deadline. → 🗣️ [Communication error].
  • 📝 The internet connection is unstable today. → 📶 [Quality issue].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Resolution (n): A solution. "What is the resolution?"
  • Escalate (v): To make more serious. "We must escalate the ticket."
  • Inconvenience (n): A trouble. "Sorry for the inconvenience."
  • Protocol (n): Official procedure. "Follow the protocol."
  • Collaborate (v): To work together. "Let's collaborate on a fix."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Work Emails: "I apologize for the delay in my response."

2. Technical Support: "My connection is unstable during video calls."

3. Business Meetings: "We had a misunderstanding regarding the budget."

title: Reported Requests
theory:
🗣️ Reported Requests (B1)

Reported requests are used to tell someone what another person politely asked. Unlike commands, requests maintain a high level of social etiquette. At the B1 level, you should be comfortable using "asked" to relay information between colleagues, friends, or family members without losing the original polite tone.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [asked] + [Person] + [(not) to + verb]

Example: "She (asked) him (to help)."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 "Can you help me?" → He asked me to help him. 🤝 [Request for aid].
  • 📝 "Could you close the window?" → She asked him to close it. 🪟 [Polite request].
  • 📝 "Please don't be late." → They asked us not to be late. ⏰ [Negative request].
  • 📝 "Will you send the file?" → He asked me to send the file. 📧 [Work request].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Politeness (n): Good manners. "Use politeness in requests."
  • Report (v): To tell what happened. "I will report what she said."
  • Inquiry (n): A question. "This is a formal inquiry."
  • Coordinate (v): To organize. "Ask him to coordinate the team."
  • Promptly (adv): Quickly. "She asked us to act promptly."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Relaying Messages: "John asked me to tell you that he's running late."

2. Workplace Tasks: "The manager asked the team to review the report."

3. Social Invitations: "They asked us to bring some drinks to the party."

John asked me ______.","a":"to call him later"},{"q":"'Please be quiet,' she asked. She asked them ______.","a":"to be quiet"}]}]}

title: Reported Commands
theory:
📢 Reported Commands (B1)

Reported commands are stronger than requests. We use the verb 'tell' or 'order' followed by the infinitive (told + person + to + verb). For example, "Sit down!" becomes "He told me to sit down." In professional settings, this is used for instructions, safety rules, and direct orders from superiors.

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 "Stop the car!" → The police told him to stop. 🚔 [Authority].
  • 📝 "Don't touch that!" → She told me not to touch it. ⚠️ [Warning/Order].
  • 📝 "Finish the work by 5." → He told us to finish by 5. 🕔 [Direct instruction].
  • 📝 "Be quiet!" → The teacher told the class to be quiet. 🤫 [Classroom order].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Authority (n): Power. "The police have authority."
  • Instruction (n): An order. "Follow the instruction."
  • Mandatory (adj): Required by rule. "This is mandatory."
  • Prohibit (v): To forbid. "He prohibited us from entering."
  • Compliance (n): Obeying a rule. "We need total compliance."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Safety Briefings: "The captain told everyone to stay seated."

2. Military/Police: "The officer told the driver to step out of the car."

3. Urgent Tasks: "My boss told me to send the email immediately."

title: Quantifiers
theory:
📊 Quantifiers: Plenty of / Several (B1)

Quantifiers tell us how much or how many. At the B1 level, we move beyond "many/much" to more natural expressions like "plenty of" (more than enough) and "several" (more than two, but not many). These help you be more precise in your descriptions of quantities in daily and business life.

🧩 Usage Guide

Plenty of: Use for both countable and uncountable nouns. (Plenty of time / Plenty of apples).

Several: Use ONLY for countable plural nouns. (Several people / Several books).

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Don't hurry, we have plenty of time. → ⏳ [More than enough].
  • 📝 I have several ideas for the new project. → 💡 [A few, but distinct].
  • 📝 There are plenty of seats in the back. → 💺 [High availability].
  • 📝 Several students were late for the lecture. → 🎓 [Small group].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Abundance (n): A large amount. "An abundance of resources."
  • Insufficient (adj): Not enough. "The funds are insufficient."
  • Portion (n): A part. "Each student gets a portion."
  • Estimate (v): To guess quantity. "Can you estimate the cost?"
  • Inventory (n): List of items. "Check the inventory."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Planning Events: "We have plenty of food for everyone."

2. Business Status: "Several clients have already signed the contract."

3. Travel: "There are plenty of flights to London every day."
