title: Narrative Tenses
description: Tell stories in the past effectively
theory:
📖 Narrative Tenses (B1)

To tell a great story or recount an event, we use four tenses together: Past Simple (main events), Past Continuous (background/setting), Past Perfect (events that happened *before* the story began), and Past Perfect Continuous (duration before an event). Mastering these allows you to create depth and sequence in your narratives.

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The sun was shining when I left the house. → ☀️ [Continuous background + Simple action].
  • 📝 I realized I had forgotten my keys. → 🔑 [Action before another past action].
  • 📝 He had been waiting for an hour before she arrived. → ⏳ [Duration before an event].
  • 📝 We were walking in the park when it started to rain. → 🌳 [Ongoing action interrupted].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Anecdote (n): A short interesting story. "Tell an anecdote."
  • Recount (v): To tell in detail. "Please recount the events."
  • Sequence (n): The order. "Keep the sequence clear."
  • Scenario (n): A possible situation. "Imagine this scenario."
  • Background (n): Setting of a story. "The background was dark."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Interviewing: "I had been working there for two years when I decided to leave."

2. Reporting News: "The witness was standing nearby when the accident happened."

3. Travel Stories: "When we arrived at the hotel, we found that they had lost our reservation."

title: Noun Phrases
theory:
✨ Complex Noun Phrases (B1)

A noun phrase is a group of words centered around a noun. At the B1 level, you learn to expand these phrases using modifiers like adjectives, prepositional phrases, and nouns as adjectives (e.g., 'a luxury hotel room'). This allows for much more descriptive and precise language, essential for professional reports and vivid storytelling.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Determiner] + [Adjective] + [Noun Modifier] + [Main Noun]

Example: "(The) (beautiful) (city) (center)."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 A high-quality digital camera. → 📸 [Complex object description].
  • 📝 The international student exchange program. → 🎓 [Abstract concept].
  • 📝 A sustainable organic vegetable garden. → 🥗 [Multi-adjective phrase].
  • 📝 That stunning view from the balcony. → 🏔️ [Noun + Prepositional phrase].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Modifier (n): A word that describes or limits another word. "Using modifiers."
  • Precision (n): The quality of being exact. "Descriptive precision."
  • Attribute (n): A quality or feature. "Nouns describing attributes."
  • Sustainable (adj): Able to be maintained. "Sustainable development."
  • Complex (adj): Consisting of many different parts. "A complex phrase."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Product Catalogs: "A reliable waterproof outdoor jacket."

2. Job Titles: "She is the Senior Marketing Project Manager."

3. Travel Guides: "A charming historic cobblestone street."

title: Future Forms Review
description: Master all ways to talk about the future
theory:
✨ Future Forms Review

This B1 level topic focuses on Future Forms Review to help you communicate with more precision and professional flair. Mastering this is a key step towards B2 fluency, allowing you to handle complex social and workplace interactions with confidence. This section covers the structure, common nuances, and practical application.

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Example sentence in context. → 🚀 [Grammar in action].
  • 📝 Professional usage example. → 💼 [Workplace relevance].
  • 📝 Daily life example. → 🏠 [Common situation].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Spontaneous: Professional term related to Future Forms Review.
  • Arrangement: Professional term related to Future Forms Review.
  • Evidence: Professional term related to Future Forms Review.
  • Certainty: Professional term related to Future Forms Review.
  • Schedule: Professional term related to Future Forms Review.
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Professional Context: Using this grammar in meetings and emails adds a level of sophistication and clarity to your communication.

2. Social Context: It helps you express complex thoughts, feelings, and hypothetical situations more naturally.

3. Academic Context: This structure is essential for writing reports and analyzing data effectively.

I've already decided.","a":"am going to buy"},{"q":"The train ______ (leave) at 10 AM.","a":"leaves"}]}]}

title: Passives Review
theory:
✨ Passives: Focus on the Action (B1)

The passive voice is used when the action itself is more important than who did it, or when the 'doer' is unknown. At the B1 level, we review the formation (be + past participle) and focus on using 'by' for agents and 'with' for instruments. This is essential for formal writing, news reports, and scientific descriptions.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Object] + [Be (appropriate tense)] + [V3 (Past Participle)]

Example: "The (cake) (was) (baked) (by) him."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The bridge was built in 1920. → 🌉 [Focus on the structure].
  • 📝 Coffee is grown in Brazil. → ☕ [General fact].
  • 📝 My car is being repaired right now. → 🚗 [Ongoing passive].
  • 📝 The letter was written with a fountain pen. → ✒️ [Focus on the tool].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Objective (adj): Not influenced by personal feelings. "A passive, objective style."
  • Assembled (v/adj): Put together. "The parts were assembled."
  • Manufactured (v): Made in a factory. "The goods were manufactured."
  • Designed (v): Created. "The logo was designed by an expert."
  • Formal (adj): Following rules or customs. "Passive is very formal."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. News Reporting: "The thief was caught after a long chase."

2. Historical Facts: "The pyramids were constructed thousands of years ago."

3. Business Processes: "Payments are processed every Monday."

Houses ______.","a":"are built"},{"q":"Shakespeare wrote Hamlet. Hamlet ______.","a":"was written"},{"q":"He is fixing the car. The car ______.","a":"is being fixed"},{"q":"They have sent the mail. The mail ______.","a":"has been sent"}]}]}

title: Purpose Clauses
theory:
🎯 Expressing Purpose (B1)

Purpose clauses explain *why* we do something. We use structures like 'to', 'in order to', and 'so that' to link actions with their goals. At the B1 level, understanding the difference between the infinitive (to + verb) and 'so that' (which starts a new clause) is vital for clear and professional communication.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Action] + [to / in order to / so that] + [Purpose]

Example: "I study (to) (learn)." / "I study (so that) (I can learn)."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I went to the shop to buy milk. → 🥛 [Simple purpose].
  • 📝 He studied hard so that he could pass. → 🎓 [Goal with clause].
  • 📝 We left early in order to avoid traffic. → 🚗 [Formal purpose].
  • 📝 I use a map so as not to get lost. → 🗺️ [Negative purpose].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Intention (n): A plan or aim. "My intention was clear."
  • Motivation (n): The reason for acting. "High motivation."
  • Streamline (v): To make more efficient. "To streamline the process."
  • Goal (n): The object of ambition. "Achieving your goal."
  • Reason (n): A cause or explanation. "The reason for calling."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Work Requests: "I'm calling to ask for your help."

2. Planning: "We save money so that we can go on holiday."

3. Education: "Read the instructions in order to understand the task."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Purpose Linkers","instruction":"Select the correct connector for the purpose.","questions":[{"q":"I'm learning English ______ get a better job.","a":"to","options":["to","for","so"],"hint":"Infinitive of purpose."},{"q":"She stayed up late ______ she could finish her essay.","a":"so that","options":["so that","in order to","to"],"hint":"Purpose clause with a subject."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"What's the Reason?","instruction":"Complete with 'to', 'in order to', or 'so that'.","questions":[{"q":"I went home ______ take a nap.","a":"to"},{"q":"He studied ______ he wouldn't fail.","a":"so that"},{"q":"We left early ______ avoid the crowd.","a":"in order to"},{"q":"I use a timer ______ I don't burn the food.","a":"so that"}]}]}

title: Verbs with Prepositions
theory:
🔗 Verb-Preposition Collocations (B1)

Many English verbs are always followed by a specific preposition (e.g., 'depend on', 'listen to', 'think about'). These fixed pairings are called collocations. At the B1 level, mastering these is essential because they don't always translate directly from other languages. Using the wrong preposition is a common mistake that can be easily fixed with practice.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Verb] + [Specific Preposition] + [Object]

Example: "I (wait) (for) (the bus)."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 It depends on the weather. → 🌦️ [Contingency].
  • 📝 She believes in your potential. → 🌟 [Confidence].
  • 📝 We succeeded in finishing the project. → ✅ [Achievement].
  • 📝 Are you listening to me? → 👂 [Attention].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Collocation (n): Words that naturally go together. "Mastering collocations."
  • Depend (v): To rely on. "Success depends on effort."
  • Succeed (v): To achieve a goal. "We succeeded in the task."
  • Believe (v): To accept as true. "I believe in honesty."
  • Listen (v): To give attention to sound. "Listen to the instructions."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Professional Emails: "Thank you for waiting for my reply."

2. Social Situations: "What are you thinking about?"

3. Problem Solving: "We need to agree on a solution."

title: Future Perfect
theory:
⏳ Future Perfect: Looking Back from the Future (B1)

The Future Perfect (will have + past participle) is used to say that an action will be completed before a specific point in the future. It's often used with 'by' or 'by the time'. Mastering this allows you to discuss deadlines, milestones, and future achievements with the precision of a professional.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Will have] + [V3 (Past Participle)]

Example: "By 5 PM, I (will) (have) (finished) (the work)."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 By 2030, I will have graduated. → 🎓 [Future milestone].
  • 📝 They will have arrived by the time we get there. → 🚗 [Sequence in the future].
  • 📝 I will have saved $1000 by Christmas. → 💰 [Financial goal].
  • 📝 The movie will have started by now. → 🎬 [Speculation about now].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Deadline (n): The latest time to finish something. "Meeting the deadline."
  • Milestone (n): A significant stage in development. "A major milestone."
  • Achievement (n): A thing done successfully. "A great achievement."
  • Completion (n): The act of finishing. "Near completion."
  • Target (n): An objective or result aimed at. "Hitting the target."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Project Management: "The building will have been completed by next June."

2. Career Goals: "I will have gained three years of experience by then."

3. Travel Arrangements: "The flight will have landed before the sun sets."

title: Get or Have Something Done
theory:
🛠️ Causative: Paying for Services (B1)

We use 'have something done' or 'get something done' to say that we arrange for someone else to do something for us. This is very common for services like repairs, cleaning, or haircuts. At the B1 level, it allows you to describe your lifestyle and professional needs with more natural syntax.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Have/Get] + [Object] + [V3 (Past Participle)]

Example: "I (had) (my car) (repaired)."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I had my hair cut yesterday. → 💇‍♂️ [Service received].
  • 📝 She got her car fixed last week. → 🚗 [Repair].
  • 📝 We need to have the house painted. → 🏠 [Arrangement].
  • 📝 He got his photo taken for the passport. → 📸 [Official task].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Arrangement (n): A plan for a future event. "Making an arrangement."
  • Service (n): The action of helping someone. "A professional service."
  • Recipient (n): A person who receives something. "The recipient of the service."
  • Contractor (n): A person who undertakes a contract. "Hiring a contractor."
  • Repair (n/v): To fix something. "The repair was successful."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Home Maintenance: "I'm having the windows cleaned tomorrow."

2. Personal Grooming: "I need to get my nails done."

3. Business Logistics: "We had the documents delivered by courier."

(My hair was cut by someone).","a":"had my hair cut","options":["had my hair cut","cut my hair","having my hair cut"],"hint":"have + object + V3"},{"q":"She needs to ______ fixed.","a":"get her car","options":["get her car","have her car","getting her car"],"hint":"get + object + V3"}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Service Request","instruction":"Complete the causative sentence.","questions":[{"q":"I had my computer ______ (repair).","a":"repaired"},{"q":"We are getting the house ______ (paint).","a":"painted"},{"q":"He got his photo ______ (take).","a":"taken"},{"q":"You should have your eyes ______ (check).","a":"checked"}]}]}

title: Should Have & Suggestions
theory:
🕰️ Should Have: Regrets & Hindsight (B1)

We use 'should have' followed by a past participle to express regret or criticism about past actions. It means something was a good idea, but it didn't happen. At the B1 level, this is essential for analyzing mistakes, giving feedback, and reflecting on personal or professional choices.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Should have] + [V3 (Past Participle)]

Example: "I (should) (have) (studied) harder."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I should have called her earlier. → 📞 [Personal regret].
  • 📝 You shouldn't have said that. → 🙊 [Criticism].
  • 📝 We should have taken a map. → 🗺️ [Logical mistake].
  • 📝 They should have arrived by now. → 🕔 [Expectation].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Regret (n/v): Feeling sad about a past action. "Deep regret."
  • Criticism (n): Expressing disapproval. "Constructive criticism."
  • Reflection (n): Serious thought. "Self-reflection."
  • Advice (n): A recommendation. "Practical advice."
  • Thoroughly (adv): Completely. "Plan thoroughly."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Performance Reviews: "You should have asked for help when you were stuck."

2. Travel Problems: "We should have checked the weather forecast."

3. Daily Life: "I should have gone to bed earlier."

(I didn't).","a":"should have studied","options":["should have studied","should study","must have studied"],"hint":"should have + V3"},{"q":"You ______ that; it was rude.","a":"shouldn't have said","options":["shouldn't have said","shouldn't say","didn't have to say"],"hint":"Negative regret."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Hindsight Lab","instruction":"Complete using 'should have' + V3.","questions":[{"q":"We ______ (take) a map.","a":"should have taken"},{"q":"I ______ (call) her earlier.","a":"should have called"},{"q":"They ______ (arrive) by now.","a":"should have arrived"},{"q":"You ______ (tell) me the truth.","a":"should have told"}]}]}

title: Would Rather vs Would Prefer
theory:
🎭 Would Rather vs Would Prefer (B1)

Both expressions are used to talk about preferences, but they have different grammatical structures. 'Would rather' is followed by the base form (no 'to'), while 'would prefer' is followed by the infinitive ('to' + verb). In professional settings, 'would prefer' sounds slightly more formal, while 'would rather' is common in direct negotiation.

🧩 Usage Guide

Would rather: I'd rather stay (Base form).

Would prefer: I'd prefer to stay (Infinitive).

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I'd rather walk than take the bus. → 🚶‍♂️ [Direct preference].
  • 📝 She'd prefer to meet at 3 PM if possible. → 🕒 [Polite arrangement].
  • 📝 Would you rather have tea or coffee? → ☕ [Offering choice].
  • 📝 I'd prefer not to discuss this now. → 🤫 [Polite refusal].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Preference (n): A choice. "What is your preference?"
  • Negotiate (v): To reach agreement. "We must negotiate a time."
  • Option (n): A choice available. "We have several options."
  • Polite (adj): Respectful. "Be polite in your requests."
  • Social (adj): Related to people. "Practice in social settings."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Business Meetings: "I would prefer to start with the budget review."

2. Casual Dining: "I'd rather sit outside than inside."

3. Travel Planning: "Would you prefer to fly direct or have a layover?"
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Preference Choice","instruction":"Choose between rather (base) and prefer (to).","questions":[{"q":"I'd rather ______ at home.","a":"stay","options":["stay","to stay","staying"],"hint":"Rather + base form."},{"q":"I'd prefer ______ out tonight.","a":"to go","options":["to go","go","going"],"hint":"Prefer + to-infinitive."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Choice Builder","instruction":"Type the correct verb form.","questions":[{"q":"Would you rather ______ (have) tea?","a":"have"},{"q":"She would prefer ______ (eat) pizza.","a":"to eat"},{"q":"I'd rather not ______ (discuss) it.","a":"discuss"},{"q":"They'd prefer ______ (walk) than drive.","a":"to walk"}]}]}

title: Third Conditional
theory:
💭 Third Conditional: Unreal Past (B1)

The Third Conditional is used to talk about things that didn't happen in the past and their hypothetical consequences. It's the grammar of 'regret' or 'imagining a different past'. Mastering this structure shows a high level of control over past perfect and modal verbs, moving you closer to B2 fluency.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[If + Past Perfect], [Subject + would have + V3]

Example: "If I (had) (known), I (would) (have) (told) you."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 If I had studied harder, I would have passed. → 🎓 [Unreal past result].
  • 📝 If we had left earlier, we wouldn't have missed the bus. → 🚌 [Different past scenario].
  • 📝 If she had seen him, she would have said hello. → 👋 [Hypothetical meeting].
  • 📝 If it hadn't rained, we would have gone to the beach. → 🏖️ [Past condition].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Hypothetical (adj): Based on an imaginary situation. "A hypothetical outcome."
  • Unreal (adj): Not real; imaginary. "An unreal past."
  • Consequence (n): A result of an action. "The consequences were clear."
  • Reflection (n): Careful thought. "Past reflection."
  • Possibility (n): Something that may happen. "A lost possibility."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Analyzing Mistakes: "If we had checked the data, the error would have been found."

2. Historical Analysis: "If the treaty had been signed, the war might not have happened."

3. Personal Regrets: "If I had taken that job, my life would have been different."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Past Impossible","instruction":"Select the correct Third Conditional form.","questions":[{"q":"If I ______ known, I ______ helped.","a":"had / would have","options":["had / would have","have / will","knew / would"],"hint":"If + past perfect, would have + V3."},{"q":"We ______ late if we ______ a taxi.","a":"wouldn't have been / had taken","options":["wouldn't have been / had taken","won't be / take","weren't / took"],"hint":"Past hypothetical."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Regret Mixer","instruction":"Complete using the Third Conditional.","questions":[{"q":"If it ______ (not rain), we would have gone.","a":"hadn't rained"},{"q":"She ______ (pass) if she had studied.","a":"would have passed"},{"q":"If I ______ (see) him, I would have said hi.","a":"had seen"},{"q":"What ______ (you/do) if you had won?","a":"would you have done"}]}]}

title: Time Clauses
theory:
⏱️ Time Clauses: Connecting Events (B1)

Time clauses use conjunctions like 'when', 'before', 'after', 'as soon as', and 'until' to show the relationship between two events. At the B1 level, the most important rule is that we use the **present simple** (not future) in a time clause referring to the future. This is a common area of confusion that distinguishes intermediate from advanced speakers.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Time Conjunction] + [Subject] + [Present Simple (for future)], [Main Clause (will)]

Example: "As soon as (he) (arrives), (I) (will) (start)."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I'll call you when I arrive. → 📞 [Future event].
  • 📝 Before you leave, please close the window. → 🪟 [Instruction].
  • 📝 We won't start until he gets here. → 🕔 [Waiting for condition].
  • 📝 I'll tell him as soon as I see him. → 🏃‍♂️ [Immediate action].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Sequence (n): The order of events. "The logical sequence."
  • Timing (n): The choice of when to act. "Perfect timing."
  • Schedule (n/v): A plan for time. "Following the schedule."
  • Immediate (adj): Happening right away. "An immediate response."
  • Limit (n/v): A point that cannot be passed. "A time limit."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Coordinating: "I'll send the files after I finish the meeting."

2. Instructions: "Don't open the door until I tell you."

3. Spontaneous Plans: "We'll go for a walk if it stops raining."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Future Timing","instruction":"Choose the correct tense for the time clause.","questions":[{"q":"I'll call you as soon as I ______.","a":"arrive","options":["arrive","will arrive","arrived"],"hint":"Time clause uses present simple for future."},{"q":"Before you ______, please lock the door.","a":"leave","options":["leave","will leave","left"],"hint":"Present simple in time clause."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Sequence Lab","instruction":"Use the Present Simple in the time clause.","questions":[{"q":"When he ______ (get) here, we will start.","a":"gets"},{"q":"I'll wait until it ______ (stop) raining.","a":"stops"},{"q":"After I ______ (finish) work, I'll go home.","a":"finish"},{"q":"As soon as she ______ (call), I'll tell you.","a":"calls"}]}]}

title: Clauses of Purpose
theory:
🎯 Clauses of Purpose: Why we act (B1)

Building on basic purpose, this topic explores more complex ways to link actions to intentions using 'so that', 'in order to', and 'so as to'. These clauses are essential for explaining strategy, motivation, and logical steps in a professional or academic environment.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Action] + [so that + subject + can/could/would]
[Action] + [in order (not) to + infinitive]

Example: "I saved money (so that) (I could buy) a car."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 He whispered so that no one would hear him. → 🤫 [Secretive purpose].
  • 📝 I use a planner in order to stay organized. → 📅 [Productivity].
  • 📝 They left early so as to avoid the rush hour. → 🚗 [Efficiency].
  • 📝 She spoke slowly so that we could understand. → 🗣️ [Clarity].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Goal (n): Target or aim. "Reaching the goal."
  • Motivation (n): Desire to do something. "Economic motivation."
  • Function (n): Purpose of something. "The function of the tool."
  • Result (n): Outcome. "A positive result."
  • Efficiency (n): Working without waste. "Improving efficiency."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Professional Motivation: "We hired more staff so that we could meet the deadline."

2. Safety Measures: "Wear a helmet in order to prevent injury."

3. Daily Habits: "I drink coffee to wake up, but I eat healthy so that I have energy."

title: Question Tags
theory:
❓ Question Tags: Confirming & Politeness (B1)

Question tags are small questions added to the end of statements (e.g., 'isn't it?', 'don't you?'). They are used to check information or to invite the listener to agree. At the B1 level, mastering the 'positive-negative' balance and auxiliary verb matching is a key indicator of natural fluency.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Positive Statement], [Negative Tag]?
[Negative Statement], [Positive Tag]?

Example: "You (are) ready, (aren't) you?" / "He (can't) go, (can) he?"

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 It's a beautiful day, isn't it? → ☀️ [Seeking agreement].
  • 📝 You haven't seen my keys, have you? → 🔑 [Checking information].
  • 📝 She speaks French, doesn't she? → 🇫🇷 [Confirmation].
  • 📝 We won't be late, will we? → 🕔 [Seeking reassurance].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Confirmation (n): Verification of something. "Seeking confirmation."
  • Agreement (n): Harmony or accordance in opinion. "Inviting agreement."
  • Auxiliary (n): A helping verb (be, do, have). "Match the auxiliary."
  • Opposite (adj): Contrary in nature. "Use the opposite polarities."
  • Interactive (adj): Involving communication. "Tags make speech interactive."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Small Talk: "Lovely weather, isn't it?"

2. Business Clarification: "The meeting is at 2 PM, isn't it?"

3. Polite Inquiries: "You don't happen to have a pen, do you?"

title: Modals of Deduction
theory:
🔍 Modals of Deduction: Solving Mysteries (B1)

We use modal verbs like 'must', 'can't', and 'might/could' to make logical guesses based on evidence. At the B1 level, you learn to express different degrees of certainty about a present situation. This is essential for analyzing problems, interpreting behavior, and discussing possibilities in both social and work contexts.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Evidence] → [Must/Can't/Might] + [Verb]

Example: "The lights are on, so they (must) (be) home."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 He has a Ferrari. He must be rich. → 🏎️ [90-100% certainty].
  • 📝 She's not answering. She might be busy. → 📱 [50% possibility].
  • 📝 They are in London. They can't be in Paris! → 🗼 [0% impossibility].
  • 📝 I'm not sure, it could be true. → 🧐 [Low certainty].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Certainty (n): Being sure of something. "Degrees of certainty."
  • Deduction (n): Logic based on evidence. "A logical deduction."
  • Evidence (n): Facts or info. "Based on the evidence."
  • Logics (n): Reasoning. "Follow the logics."
  • Inference (n): A conclusion. "Make an inference."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Problem Solving: "The server is down; there must be a power cut."

2. Interpreting Emotions: "She's smiling; she might be happy about the news."

3. Daily Mysteries: "I can't find my keys; they must be in my other bag."

They ______ be home.","a":"must","options":["must","can't","might"],"hint":"High certainty (evidence)."},{"q":"He's in London. He ______ be in Paris!","a":"can't","options":["can't","must","might"],"hint":"Impossibility."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Deduction Lab","instruction":"Use: must, can't, or might.","questions":[{"q":"She's not answering. She ______ be busy.","a":"might"},{"q":"I'm 100% sure. It ______ be true.","a":"must"},{"q":"It's impossible. He ______ be the thief.","a":"can't"},{"q":"I don't know, it ______ rain later.","a":"might"}]}]}
premium_practice: {"games":[{"type":"matching","title":"Deduction Matcher 🧩","questions":[{"q":"100% Sure Yes","a":"Must"},{"q":"100% Sure No","a":"Can't"},{"q":"Maybe / Not Sure","a":"Might / Could"},{"q":"Probably","a":"Must"},{"q":"Impossible","a":"Can't"}]}]}

title: Past Modals of Deduction
description: Make logical conclusions about the past
theory:
🕰️ Past Deduction: must have / can't have (B1)

To make logical guesses about the *past*, we use 'modal + have + past participle'. This is a step up from present deduction and is vital for investigating events, explaining past mistakes, or speculating about history. It combines modal logic with the perfect aspect.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Evidence] → [Must/Can't/Might] + [have] + [V3]

Example: "The ground is wet. It (must) (have) (rained)."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 He looks tired. He must have worked late. → 😴 [Past certainty].
  • 📝 The cake is gone! Someone must have eaten it. → 🍰 [Deduction of action].
  • 📝 She can't have known about the party; it was a secret. → 🤫 [Past impossibility].
  • 📝 I might have left my keys in the office. → 🔑 [Past possibility].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Speculation (n): Forming a theory without firm evidence. "Pure speculation."
  • Hindsight (n): Understanding a situation only after it happened. "In hindsight."
  • Verification (n): Proving something is true. "Needs verification."
  • Context (n): The circumstances. "The past context."
  • Probability (n): Likelihood. "High probability."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Crime Investigation: "The thief must have entered through the back door."

2. Historical Theories: "The city might have been destroyed by a volcano."

3. Resolving Conflicts: "He can't have understood the instructions correctly."

I ______ them at work.","a":"might have left","options":["might have left","must leave","can't have left"],"hint":"Past possibility."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Investigation Room","instruction":"Complete using: must have, can't have, or might have.","questions":[{"q":"He looks happy. He ______ (know) the code.","a":"must have known"},{"q":"She ______ (forget); she's very reliable.","a":"can't have forgotten"},{"q":"I ______ (leave) my wallet on the bus.","a":"might have left"}]}]}

title: Reported Speech
theory:
✨ B1 Grammar Point

This B1 level topic focuses on advanced structures to help you communicate with more precision and professional flair. Mastering this is a key step towards B2 fluency, allowing you to handle complex social and workplace interactions with confidence. This section covers the structure, common nuances, and practical application.

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Example sentence in context. → 🚀 [Grammar in action].
  • 📝 Professional usage example. → 💼 [Workplace relevance].
  • 📝 Daily life example. → 🏠 [Common situation].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Shift: Professional term related to this topic.
  • Tense: Professional term related to this topic.
  • Relay: Professional term related to this topic.
  • Journalism: Professional term related to this topic.
  • Statement: Professional term related to this topic.
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Professional Context: Using this grammar in meetings and emails adds a level of sophistication and clarity to your communication.

2. Social Context: It helps you express complex thoughts, feelings, and hypothetical situations more naturally.

3. Academic Context: This structure is essential for writing reports and analyzing data effectively.

title: Conditionals Review
theory:
🧠 Conditionals: Logical Scenarios (B2)

At the B2 level, you must master the logic of Zero, First, Second, and Third Conditionals. This review focuses on selecting the right structure based on the probability and time of the scenario. Whether you are discussing scientific facts (Zero), future plans (First), imaginary dreams (Second), or past regrets (Third), precision in tense selection is key to sophisticated English.

🧩 Grammar Quick-Reference
  • Zero: If + Present, Present. (Facts).
  • First: If + Present, Will. (Likely future).
  • Second: If + Past, Would. (Imaginary present).
  • Third: If + Past Perfect, Would have. (Past regret).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 If it rains, I stay home. → 🌧️ [General fact].
  • 📝 If I win, I will buy a car. → 🚗 [Real possibility].
  • 📝 If I were you, I would go. → 💭 [Imaginary advice].
  • 📝 If I had known, I would have helped. → 🤝 [Past reflection].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Hypothetical (adj): Imaginary. "A hypothetical plan."
  • Consequence (n): Result. "Analyze the consequences."
  • Probability (n): Likelihood. "Calculate the probability."
  • Regret (n): Sadness over the past. "No regrets."
  • Scenario (n): Situation. "Imagine a scenario."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Strategy: "If we increase the budget, we will achieve more."

2. Negotiations: "If you lowered the price, we would sign the deal."

3. Problem Solving: "If they had checked the code, it wouldn't have crashed."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Conditional Master","instruction":"Select the correct conditional type.","questions":[{"q":"If I ______ (have) more time, I would learn French.","a":"had","options":["had","have","would have"],"hint":"Second conditional: If + Past."},{"q":"If we ______ (finish) early, we'll go for a walk.","a":"finish","options":["finish","will finish","finished"],"hint":"First conditional: If + Present."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Complex Scenarios","instruction":"Complete using the correct conditional form.","questions":[{"q":"If I ______ (see) him yesterday, I ______ (say) hello.","a":"had seen, would have said"},{"q":"If it ______ (snow) tonight, we ______ (stay) in.","a":"snows, will stay"},{"q":"What ______ (you/do) if you ______ (be) the president?","a":"would you do, were"},{"q":"He ______ (not be) late if he ______ (leave) on time.","a":"wouldn't have been, had left"}]}]}

title: Reported Speech
theory:
🗣️ Reported Speech: Relaying Messages (B2)

Reporting what someone said requires 'backshifting' tenses (e.g., Present becomes Past, Will becomes Would). At the B2 level, you also need to handle complex time changes (today → that day) and reporting verbs beyond 'said' and 'told', such as 'claimed', 'denied', or 'warned'. This is critical for professional communication and journalism.

🧩 Tense Backshift Rules
  • Am/Is/Are → Was/Were
  • Will → Would
  • Can → Could
  • Past Simple → Past Perfect
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 "I'm busy" → He said he was busy. 📱 [Message relay].
  • 📝 "I'll call you" → She said she would call. 📞 [Promise].
  • 📝 "I've finished" → He told me he had finished. ✅ [Update].
  • 📝 "Go home!" → He told us to go home. 🏠 [Order].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Backshift (v): To change a tense backwards. "Remember to backshift."
  • Relay (v): To pass on information. "Relay the message."
  • Confirm (v): To state that something is true. "He confirmed it."
  • Deny (v): To say something is not true. "She denied the claim."
  • Warn (v): To tell of danger. "They warned us."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Corporate Reporting: "The CEO announced that profits had increased."

2. Summarizing Meetings: "John suggested that we postpone the launch."

3. Daily Gossip: "She told me that they were getting married."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Reported Relay","instruction":"Select the correct reported form.","questions":[{"q":"'I am tired,' he said.","a":"He said he was tired.","options":["He said he was tired.","He said I am tired.","He told he was tired."],"hint":"Present simple becomes past simple."},{"q":"'I will help you,' she told me.","a":"She told me she would help me.","options":["She told me she would help me.","She told me she will help me.","She said me she would help."],"hint":"Will becomes would."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Indirect Quotes","instruction":"Transform into reported speech.","questions":[{"q":"'I have finished.' → He said he ______.","a":"had finished"},{"q":"'We can come.' → They said they ______.","a":"could come"},{"q":"'I'll be there.' → She said she ______.","a":"would be there"},{"q":"'I went home.' → He told me he ______.","a":"had gone home"}]}]}
premium_practice: {"games":[{"type":"matching","title":"Verb Backshift Match 🧩","questions":[{"q":"Is/Am...","a":"...Was"},{"q":"Will...","a":"...Would"},{"q":"Have...","a":"...Had"},{"q":"Can...","a":"...Could"},{"q":"Present Simple...","a":"...Past Simple"}]}]}

title: Relative Clauses
theory:
🔗 Relative Clauses: Adding Detail (B2)

Relative clauses allow you to combine sentences and add essential or extra information about a noun without starting a new sentence. At the B2 level, we focus on the distinction between Defining (essential) and Non-defining (extra) clauses, and using relative pronouns like 'whom', 'whose', and 'where' with total accuracy.

🧩 Pronoun Usage
  • Who/Whom: People.
  • Which: Things.
  • That: People/Things (Defining only).
  • Whose: Possession.
  • Where: Places.
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The man who lives next door is nice. → 🏘️ [Essential info].
  • 📝 Paris, which is famous for art, is big. → 🗼 [Extra info with commas].
  • 📝 That is the girl whose father is a pilot. → ✈️ [Possession].
  • 📝 The office where I work is modern. → 💼 [Place].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Defining (adj): Necessary to identify the noun. "A defining clause."
  • Non-defining (adj): Extra, unnecessary info. "Use commas for non-defining."
  • Omit (v): To leave out. "You can omit the pronoun if it's the object."
  • Attribute (v): To give credit or quality. "Information attributed to him."
  • Clarify (v): To make clear. "Clauses clarify who we mean."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Technical Specs: "The sensor which detects motion is calibrated."

2. Biographies: "Shakespeare, whose plays are world-famous, was born in Stratford."

3. Business Location: "The city where we opened our branch is growing."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Link the Noun","instruction":"Select the correct relative pronoun.","questions":[{"q":"The woman ______ called me is my boss.","a":"who","options":["who","which","whose"],"hint":"Use 'who' for people."},{"q":"That is the house ______ I was born.","a":"where","options":["where","which","that"],"hint":"Use 'where' for places."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Defining the Details","instruction":"Complete with: who, which, where, whose, whom.","questions":[{"q":"The company ______ I work for is global.","a":"which"},{"q":"That's the man ______ car was stolen.","a":"whose"},{"q":"The city ______ we met is beautiful.","a":"where"},{"q":"The candidate ______ we chose is excellent.","a":"whom"}]}]}
premium_practice: {"games":[{"type":"matching","title":"Pronoun Matcher 🧩","questions":[{"q":"People","a":"Who / Whom"},{"q":"Things","a":"Which"},{"q":"Places","a":"Where"},{"q":"Possession","a":"Whose"},{"q":"Essential (no commas)","a":"That"}]}]}

title: Modals of Ability
theory:
💪 Modals of Ability: Can, Could, Be Able To (B2)

Expressing ability involves more than just 'can'. At the B2 level, we distinguish between general ability ('could') and specific success in a past situation ('was able to' or 'managed to'). We also explore future ability using 'will be able to'. Understanding these nuances is key for professional storytelling and resume building.

🧩 Usage Guidelines
  • Can: Present general ability.
  • Could: Past general ability.
  • Was able to: Specific past success (one-off event).
  • Will be able to: Future ability.
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I can speak three languages. → 🗣️ [General skill].
  • 📝 He could swim when he was five. → 🏊‍♂️ [Past general skill].
  • 📝 Although it was hard, I was able to finish. → ✅ [Specific success].
  • 📝 One day, we will be able to travel to Mars. → 🚀 [Future potential].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Competence (n): The ability to do something successfully. "Professional competence."
  • Potential (n): Latent qualities that may be developed. "Unlock your potential."
  • Efficiently (adv): In a way that achieves maximum productivity. "Work efficiently."
  • Mastery (n): Comprehensive knowledge or skill. "Achieve mastery."
  • Acquire (v): To buy or obtain. "Acquire a new skill."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Interviews: "I was able to increase sales by 20% in my last role."

2. Planning: "After the training, you will be able to use the software."

3. Childhood: "I couldn't ride a bike until I was ten."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Ability Choice","instruction":"Select the correct modal of ability.","questions":[{"q":"The exam was very difficult, but I ______ finish it.","a":"was able to","options":["was able to","could","can"],"hint":"Specific past success."},{"q":"By next year, I ______ speak fluently.","a":"will be able to","options":["will be able to","could","can"],"hint":"Future ability."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Skill Assessment","instruction":"Complete with: can, could, was able to, will be able to.","questions":[{"q":"When I was young, I ______ run very fast.","a":"could"},{"q":"I ______ (finally) solve the puzzle after an hour.","a":"was able to"},{"q":"If you practice, you ______ speak well.","a":"will be able to"},{"q":"I ______ see the mountains from my window.","a":"can"}]}]}
premium_practice: {"games":[{"type":"matching","title":"Ability Matcher 🧩","questions":[{"q":"Present Skill","a":"Can"},{"q":"Past General Skill","a":"Could"},{"q":"Specific Past Success","a":"Was able to"},{"q":"Future Potential","a":"Will be able to"},{"q":"Lack of Ability","a":"Cannot / Unable to"}]}]}

title: Modals of Obligation
theory:
🚨 Obligation & Necessity: Must, Have to, Should (B2)

Expressing rules and advice requires choosing between internal obligation ('must'), external rules ('have to'), and recommendations ('should'). At the B2 level, we also cover the difference between 'mustn't' (prohibition) and 'don't have to' (lack of necessity), which is one of the most common pitfalls in English grammar.

🧩 Rule Breakdown
  • Must: Personal obligation or very strong rule.
  • Have to: External rule (laws, work rules).
  • Mustn't: It is forbidden.
  • Don't have to: It is not necessary (but you can if you want).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 You must come to the party! → 🎉 [Strong personal urge].
  • 📝 I have to wear a uniform at work. → 👔 [External rule].
  • 📝 You mustn't smoke here. → 🚭 [Prohibition].
  • 📝 You don't have to pay; it's free. → 🆓 [Lack of necessity].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Mandatory (adj): Required by law or rules. "Attendance is mandatory."
  • Prohibit (v): To forbid. "Smoking is prohibited."
  • Optional (adj): Left to one's choice. "This step is optional."
  • Compliance (n): Obeying a rule or order. "Ensure total compliance."
  • Urgency (n): Importance requiring swift action. "A matter of urgency."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Workplace Safety: "All staff must wear safety goggles."

2. Travel: "You don't have to have a visa for this country."

3. Personal Advice: "You should see that new movie; it's great!"
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Rule Checker","instruction":"Select the correct modal for the situation.","questions":[{"q":"Tomorrow is a holiday, so I ______ go to work.","a":"don't have to","options":["don't have to","mustn't","shouldn't"],"hint":"Lack of necessity."},{"q":"You ______ park here; it's against the law.","a":"mustn't","options":["mustn't","don't have to","shouldn't"],"hint":"Prohibition."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Necessity & Prohibition","instruction":"Complete with: must, mustn't, have to, don't have to.","questions":[{"q":"In the UK, you ______ drive on the left.","a":"have to"},{"q":"You ______ tell anyone; it's a secret.","a":"mustn't"},{"q":"I ______ (leave) now or I'll miss my train.","a":"must"},{"q":"You ______ wash the dishes; I'll do it later.","a":"don't have to"}]}]}

title: Verbs + Gerunds
description: Master verbs that take gerund forms
theory:
⏳ Verbs + Gerunds (B2)

At the Upper-Intermediate level, you must master verbs that require the gerund form (-ing) to express attitudes, intentions, and ongoing professional processes. Unlike B1, B2 focuses on verbs like 'contemplate', 'resent', and 'postpone', which are common in executive discussions and complex social interactions. Understanding these nuances is essential for sounding authoritative and precise.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [B2 Level Verb (consider/deny)] + [Gerund (Verb-ing)]

Example: "We (postponed) (signing) the contract."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 We are considering expanding our business to Europe. → 💼 [Strategic planning].
  • 📝 The CEO denied making those comments to the press. → 📰 [Official statement].
  • 📝 She postponed meeting with the clients until next week. → 📅 [Schedule change].
  • 📝 I resent being treated like a child in the office. → 😡 [Strong emotion].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Contemplate (v): To think deeply about. "I'm contemplating changing jobs."
  • Anticipate (v): To expect. "We anticipate seeing growth this year."
  • Postpone (v): To delay. "Let's postpone signing the contract."
  • Resent (v): To feel bitterness. "They resent paying higher taxes."
  • Dread (v): To fear. "I dread giving presentations."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Corporate Strategy: "Management is considering merging with our competitor."

2. Legal Contexts: "The suspect denied being at the scene of the crime."

3. Project Management: "We cannot avoid addressing these technical issues any longer."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"B2 Verb Patterns","instruction":"Choose the correct verb form.","questions":[{"q":"I ______ going to the cinema on Fridays.","a":"enjoy","options":["enjoy","want","hope"],"hint":"Enjoy is followed by -ing."},{"q":"He ______ stealing the money.","a":"denied","options":["denied","refused","promised"],"hint":"Denied takes the gerund form."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Gerund Focus","instruction":"Type the gerund form of the verb in brackets.","questions":[{"q":"She avoids ______ (talk) about her ex.","a":"talking"},{"q":"We considered ______ (move) to Canada.","a":"moving"},{"q":"I finished ______ (read) the report.","a":"reading"},{"q":"They suggest ______ (wait) until tomorrow.","a":"waiting"}]}]}

However, I avoid {{1}} coffee before bed. My friend suggested {{2}} a yoga class. I am considering {{3}} the gym next week.","answers":["running","drinking","joining","entering"],"distractors":["to run","drink","join"]}]}

title: Passive Voice
description: Use noun clauses to explain and define
theory:
🏗️ Passive Voice: Professional Precision (B2)

The passive voice shifts the focus from the 'doer' to the 'action'. At the B2 level, you must be able to use the passive in all tenses, including continuous and perfect forms. This is the language of formal reports, academic papers, and technical manuals where objectivity is more important than personal perspective.

🧩 Tense Transformation
  • Present Continuous: is/are being + V3.
  • Past Perfect: had been + V3.
  • Future: will be + V3.
  • Modals: can/must be + V3.
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The report is being written now. → 📑 [Present ongoing].
  • 📝 The decision had already been made. → ⚖️ [Past perfect].
  • 📝 New laws will be introduced soon. → 📜 [Future].
  • 📝 This problem must be solved. → 💡 [Modal].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Objective (adj): Based on facts rather than feelings. "An objective tone."
  • Attribute (v): To assign a cause. "The success was attributed to the team."
  • Analyze (v): To examine in detail. "Data must be analyzed."
  • Consolidate (v): To combine into a whole. "The results were consolidated."
  • Implement (v): To put into effect. "Changes will be implemented."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. News Media: "The building was destroyed by a fire."

2. Manuals: "The button must be pressed for three seconds."

3. Business History: "The company was founded in 1990."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Passive Tense Choice","instruction":"Select the correctly formed passive tense.","questions":[{"q":"The documents ______ by the time we arrived.","a":"had been signed","options":["had been signed","were signed","have been signed"],"hint":"Past Perfect Passive."},{"q":"A new stadium ______ next year.","a":"will be built","options":["will be built","is built","was built"],"hint":"Future Simple Passive."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Advanced Passives","instruction":"Complete the passive sentences.","questions":[{"q":"The bridge ______ (construct) in 1890.","a":"was constructed"},{"q":"The patient ______ (examine) by the doctor right now.","a":"is being examined"},{"q":"Mistakes ______ (make) by the team last week.","a":"were made"},{"q":"The winner ______ (announce) tomorrow.","a":"will be announced"}]}]}

title: Advanced Comparison
theory:
⚖️ Advanced Comparison & Contrast (B2)

Beyond 'taller' and 'better', B2 level English uses sophisticated structures like 'as...as', 'the more...the more', and modifiers like 'far more', 'slightly', or 'nowhere near'. These tools allow you to compare complex data, evaluate business options, and express preferences with extreme precision.

🧩 Comparison Modifiers
  • Large difference: Far more, way more, significantly.
  • Small difference: Slightly, a bit, marginally.
  • Negative comparison: Nowhere near as... as.
  • Parallel increase: The (more)... the (better).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 This model is far more efficient than the old one. → ⚡ [Huge improvement].
  • 📝 The results were slightly better than expected. → 📈 [Small margin].
  • 📝 The harder you work, the luckier you get. → 🍀 [Direct correlation].
  • 📝 It is nowhere near as expensive as Paris. → 💰 [Significant cost difference].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Correlation (n): A mutual relationship. "A positive correlation."
  • Significantly (adv): In a sufficiently great or important way. "Significantly higher."
  • Marginal (adj): Minor or not central. "A marginal gain."
  • Equivalent (adj): Equal in value or function. "The equivalent price."
  • Discrepancy (n): A lack of similarity. "A large discrepancy."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Data Analysis: "Q4 was marginally better than Q3, but far worse than Q2."

2. Reviews: "The new phone is nowhere near as good as the reviews say."

3. Advice: "The sooner we start, the earlier we finish."
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Comparison Modifiers","instruction":"Select the most appropriate comparison modifier.","questions":[{"q":"This house is ______ as expensive as that mansion.","a":"nowhere near","options":["nowhere near","far","slightly"],"hint":"Negative equality."},{"q":"The new software is ______ more reliable.","a":"significantly","options":["significantly","much as","as"],"hint":"Adverb of degree."}]},{"type":"fill_in","title":"Degree of Difference","instruction":"Complete with: far, slightly, nowhere near, more.","questions":[{"q":"Today is ______ hotter than yesterday (just 1 degree).","a":"slightly"},{"q":"China is ______ bigger than the UK.","a":"far"},{"q":"The harder you study, the ______ you learn.","a":"more"},{"q":"My car is ______ as fast as a Ferrari.","a":"nowhere near"}]}]}

title: Modal Uses
theory:
🔍 Modal Uses: Degrees of Certainty (B2)

At the B2 level, we use modal verbs to express precise degrees of certainty about current and future events. This involves moving beyond basic permission and ability to nuanced deduction. Understanding the difference between 'could', 'might', and 'may' is essential for sophisticated communication.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Subject] + [Modal] + [Verb (Base Form)]

Example: "It (could) (be) true, but I doubt it."

📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Probability (n): Likelihood.
  • Certainty (n): State of being sure.
  • Inference (n): Logical conclusion.
  • Speculation (n): Guessing without proof.
practice: {"games":[{"type":"multiple_choice","title":"Habit, Advice, or Ability?","instruction":"Determine the use of the modal in the sentence.","questions":[{"q":"He would always bring me flowers. (Usage?)","options":["Past Habit","Advice","Possibility"],"a":"Past Habit","hint":"Consider the B2 nuances: look for formal markers or specific tense sequences."},{"q":"You should see a doctor. (Usage?)","options":["Advice","Obligation","Deduction"],"a":"Advice","hint":"Consider the B2 nuances: look for formal markers or specific tense sequences."},{"q":"I can play the flute. (Usage?)","options":["Ability","Permission","Possibility"],"a":"Ability","hint":"Consider the B2 nuances: look for formal markers or specific tense sequences."},{"q":"You must wear a seatbelt. title: Countable & Uncountable
theory:
🍎 Countable vs Uncountable Nouns (B2)

Distinguishing between countable and uncountable nouns is fundamental for natural English. At the B2 level, we focus on 'tricky' nouns (advice, news, furniture, research) and 'dual-use' nouns that change meaning (paper, hair, business). Mastering the correct quantifiers and determiners for these nouns is essential for academic accuracy and professional writing.

🧩 Advanced Noun Rules
  • Uncountable (Always): Information, Advice, News, Research, Furniture, Progress.
  • Dual-Use: A business (company) vs Business (activity).
  • Dual-Use: A glass (object) vs Glass (material).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I need some advice on the project. → 💡 [Uncountable - no 'an'].
  • 📝 The news was shocking. → 🗞️ [Singular verb required].
  • 📝 He has done a lot of research. → 🧪 [Uncountable].
  • 📝 She started a successful business. → 🏢 [Countable].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Quantity (n): An amount or number of something. "Measuring the quantity."
  • Information (n): Facts provided or learned. "Accurate information."
  • Progress (n): Forward movement toward a destination. "Steady progress."
  • Furniture (n): Large movable equipment. "Office furniture."
  • Segment (n/v): A separate part. "A segment of the data."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Work Reports: "We have made great progress this month."

2. Academic Writing: "The research indicates that..." (Never 'researches').

3. Travel: "The furniture in the hotel was very modern."

title: Order of Modifiers
theory:
✨ Order of Modifiers (B2)

When using multiple adjectives to describe a noun, English follows a strict natural order. Mastering this sequence (Opinion, Size, Age, Shape, Color, Origin, Material, Purpose) makes your English sound significantly more native and professional.

🧩 The OSASCOMP Rule

Opinion, Size, Age, Shape, Color, Origin, Material, Purpose.

Example: "A (beautiful) (large) (old) (wooden) desk."

title: Passive Voice Past
theory:
🏗️ Passive Voice Past (B2)

The Passive Voice in the past (was/were + V3) is used to report historical events, scientific results, or actions where the agent is unknown or unimportant. At the B2 level, we focus on maintaining objectivity in professional reporting.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Object] + [Was / Were] + [V3 (Past Participle)]

Example: "The building (was) (constructed) in 1920."

title: Infinitive Patterns
theory:
🔗 Infinitive Patterns (B2)

Many English verbs and adjectives are followed specifically by the to-infinitive. At the B2 level, we explore complex patterns like 'too/enough' with infinitives and verbs like 'decide', 'promise', and 'expect'.

🧩 Key Structures
  • Verb + to-inf: I hope to see you.
  • Too + Adj + to-inf: It's too hot to run.
  • Adj + enough + to-inf: He's old enough to drive.

title: Noun-forming Suffixes
theory:
✨ Noun-forming Suffixes: Academic Precision (B2)

In B2 English, you need to transform verbs and adjectives into nouns to sound more formal and objective. Suffixes like '-tion', '-ity', '-ment', and '-ness' are the building blocks of academic and professional vocabulary. Understanding how to use these correctly allows you to discuss abstract concepts like 'implementation', 'prosperity', and 'resilience' with ease.

🧩 Common Suffix Patterns
  • -tion / -sion: Verbs to Nouns (Educate → Education).
  • -ment: Verbs to Nouns (Develop → Development).
  • -ity / -ty: Adjectives to Nouns (Possible → Possibility).
  • -ness: Adjectives to Nouns (Happy → Happiness).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 The implementation of the plan was successful. → 🚀 [Action].
  • 📝 We value creativity in our team. → 🎨 [Quality].
  • 📝 The management decided to hire more staff. → 👔 [Group/Process].
  • 📝 Your kindness is much appreciated. → ❤️ [Trait].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Prosperity (n): State of being successful. "Economic prosperity."
  • Resilience (n): Ability to recover quickly. "Emotional resilience."
  • Implementation (n): Putting a plan into effect. "Swift implementation."
  • Cognition (n): Process of acquiring knowledge. "Human cognition."
  • Magnitude (n): Great size or extent. "A project of this magnitude."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Business Reports: "The development of the new software took six months."

2. Academic Essays: "The importance of education cannot be overstated."

3. News: "The reduction in inflation is a positive sign for the economy."

title: Tag Questions
theory:
❓ Tag Questions: Confirming & Engaging (B2)

Tag questions are small questions at the end of statements (e.g., '..., aren't you?'). They are used to check information or ask for agreement. At the B2 level, we focus on trickier tags like 'I am → aren't I?', imperatives ('Open the door, will you?'), and maintaining the correct intonation for different meanings.

🧩 The Logic of Opposites
  • Positive Statement → Negative Tag: You are ready, aren't you?
  • Negative Statement → Positive Tag: You haven't seen it, have you?
  • Auxiliary Match: Use 'do/does' if no auxiliary is present.
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 You like pizza, don't you? → 🍕 [Seeking agreement].
  • 📝 He hasn't called, has he? → 📞 [Checking info].
  • 📝 I am late, aren't I? → ⏰ [Irregular].
  • 📝 Let's go, shall we? → 🚶‍♂️ [Suggestion].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Affirmation (n): Emotional support or encouragement. "Seeking affirmation."
  • Interrogative (adj): Having the force of a question. "An interrogative tone."
  • Rhetorical (adj): Asked to make a point rather than get an answer. "A rhetorical tag."
  • Implicit (adj): Suggested though not directly expressed. "An implicit agreement."
  • Validate (v): To check or prove the validity. "To validate a statement."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Socializing: "It's a beautiful day, isn't it?"

2. Workplace: "You finished the report, didn't you?"

3. Giving Orders: "Don't forget the keys, will you?"

title: Adjective-forming Suffixes
theory:
✨ Adjective-forming Suffixes (B2 Mastery)

In the Upper-Intermediate level (B2), precision is paramount. This topic focuses on complex structures that allow you to express subtle shades of meaning, formal intentions, and sophisticated arguments. Mastering this will bridge the gap between being a functional speaker and a truly fluent communicator in professional and academic environments.

🎨 Visual Examples & Nuances
  • 📝 Specialized usage in a professional context. → 💼 [Professional Flair].
  • 📝 Complex sentence structure example. → 🧠 [Cognitive Depth].
  • 📝 Everyday advanced interaction. → 🤝 [Social Sophistication].
📚 Academic & Professional Vocabulary
  • Abundant: Advanced B2 term related to Adjective-forming Suffixes.
  • Substantial: Advanced B2 term related to Adjective-forming Suffixes.
  • Pragmatic: Advanced B2 term related to Adjective-forming Suffixes.
  • Intuitive: Advanced B2 term related to Adjective-forming Suffixes.
  • Diverse: Advanced B2 term related to Adjective-forming Suffixes.
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Executive Communication: High-level management uses these structures to project authority and clarity in strategic reports.

2. Academic Writing: Essential for university-level essays where you must analyze evidence and present balanced viewpoints.

3. Social Fluency: Allows you to engage in deep discussions about culture, politics, and abstract concepts with native-like ease.

title: Reported Speech: Advanced (B2)
theory:
🗣️ Reported Speech: Beyond the Basics (B2)

Reported speech at B2 involves more than just changing 'is' to 'was'. We focus on reporting questions (if/whether), commands (told to), and shifting time expressions (tomorrow → the next day). Mastering this allows you to relay complex conversations and professional updates with absolute precision.

🧩 The Backshift Rule
  • Present Simple → Past Simple: "I work" → He said he worked.
  • Present Perfect → Past Perfect: "I have gone" → She said she had gone.
  • Will → Would: "I will help" → They said they would help.
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 "I'm busy," he said. → He said he was busy. → 🏃‍♂️ [Backshift].
  • 📝 "Have you seen it?" → He asked if I had seen it. → ❓ [Question].
  • 📝 "Wait here!" → She told me to wait there. → ⏳ [Command].
  • 📝 "I'll be there tomorrow." → He said he'd be there the next day. → 📅 [Time shift].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Convey (v): To transport or communicate. "To convey a message."
  • Assertion (n): A confident statement of fact. "A strong assertion."
  • Recount (v): To tell someone about something. "To recount a story."
  • Disclose (v): To make known. "To disclose information."
  • Transcribe (v): To put into written form. "To transcribe a speech."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. News Reporting: "The President stated that the economy was improving."

2. Legal: "The witness claimed that she had seen the incident."

3. Business: "She asked whether the meeting had been cancelled."

title: Participial Adjectives
theory:
✨ -ed vs -ing: Feelings vs Causes (B2)

Participial adjectives help us describe how we feel or the cause of a feeling. At the B2 level, we move beyond 'bored/boring' to more descriptive terms like 'daunted/daunting', 'exhilarated/exhilarating', and 'intrigued/intriguing'. The rule is simple: -ed describes the person's feeling, while -ing describes the thing causing it.

🧩 The Core Logic
  • Person + -ed: I am intrigued.
  • Situation + -ing: This book is intriguing.
  • Exceptions: Be careful with people being 'boring' (their personality).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 It was an exhilarating experience. → 🎢 [Cause].
  • 📝 I felt exhilarated after the ride. → 😄 [Feeling].
  • 📝 The complexity was overwhelming. → 🌊 [Cause].
  • 📝 He was overwhelmed by the work. → 😵 [Feeling].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Exhilarating (adj): Very exciting and happy. "An exhilarating game."
  • Daunting (adj): Intimidating or scary. "A daunting task."
  • Intriguing (adj): Mysterious or fascinating. "An intriguing idea."
  • Perplexing (adj): Confusing or puzzling. "A perplexing mystery."
  • Devastating (adj): Extremely shocking. "Devastating news."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Travel Reviews: "The view was stunning, and we were amazed."

2. Work Pressure: "The workload is tiring, and I feel exhausted."

3. Media: "It was a shocking documentary about the environment."

title: Unreal Conditionals
theory:
💭 Second & Third Conditionals: Imagining (B2)

Unreal conditionals allow us to talk about situations that are not real or didn't happen. At the B2 level, we review the Second Conditional (imaginary present) and the Third Conditional (imaginary past). Mastering these structures is vital for negotiation, problem analysis, and expressing deep regrets or future dreams.

🧩 Comparison Table
  • Second: If I had money, I would buy it. (Now).
  • Third: If I had had money, I would have bought it. (Past).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 If I were you, I wouldn't do that. → 💭 [Advice].
  • 📝 If we had known, we would have called. → 📞 [Past regret].
  • 📝 I would travel more if I had more holidays. → ✈️ [Present dream].
  • 📝 He wouldn't have failed if he had studied. → 🎓 [Past logic].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Hypothetical (adj): Based on an imaginary situation. "A hypothetical plan."
  • Conjecture (n): A guess based on incomplete info. "Scientific conjecture."
  • Improbable (adj): Not likely to happen. "An improbable outcome."
  • Contemplation (n): Deep thought. "Moments of contemplation."
  • Idealistic (adj): Aiming for perfection. "An idealistic view."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Executive Planning: "If the market crashed, we would lose millions."

2. Academic Theory: "If the planet were closer to the sun, life would be impossible."

3. Personal Logic: "I would have helped if you had asked."

title: Indirect Questions
theory:
🤝 Indirect Questions: Diplomacy & Etiquette (B2)

Indirect questions are more polite and formal than direct ones. We use phrases like 'Could you tell me...?' or 'I wonder if...'. The most important B2 rule: the word order returns to a statement (Subject + Verb) and we do NOT use 'do/does/did'. This is the standard for professional and academic interaction.

🧩 Word Order Shift
  • Direct: Where is the bank?
  • Indirect: Do you know where the bank is?
  • Direct: Why did he leave?
  • Indirect: Can you tell me why he left?
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Could you tell me what time it starts? → 🕒 [Polite inquiry].
  • 📝 I wonder if she is coming. → ❓ [Doubt/Speculation].
  • 📝 Do you have any idea where they went? → 🗺️ [Searching].
  • 📝 I'd like to know how much it costs. → 💰 [Formal request].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Diplomacy (n): Skill in managing negotiations. "Use diplomacy in tone."
  • Etiquette (n): Code of polite behavior. "Follow business etiquette."
  • Inquiry (n): An official request for info. "A formal inquiry."
  • Discretion (n): Care in speech or action. "Use discretion when asking."
  • Politeness (n): Good manners. "Maintain politeness."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Office Context: "Could you tell me where the meeting is?"

2. Customer Service: "I was wondering if you had any rooms available."

3. Street Directions: "Excuse me, do you know where the library is?"

title: Gerunds vs Infinitives
theory:
✨ Gerunds vs Infinitives (B2 Mastery)

In the Upper-Intermediate level (B2), precision is paramount. This topic focuses on complex structures that allow you to express subtle shades of meaning, formal intentions, and sophisticated arguments. Mastering this will bridge the gap between being a functional speaker and a truly fluent communicator in professional and academic environments.

🎨 Visual Examples & Nuances
  • 📝 Specialized usage in a professional context. → 💼 [Professional Flair].
  • 📝 Complex sentence structure example. → 🧠 [Cognitive Depth].
  • 📝 Everyday advanced interaction. → 🤝 [Social Sophistication].
📚 Academic & Professional Vocabulary
  • Contrast: Advanced B2 term related to Gerunds vs Infinitives.
  • Intent: Advanced B2 term related to Gerunds vs Infinitives.
  • Preference: Advanced B2 term related to Gerunds vs Infinitives.
  • Consistency: Advanced B2 term related to Gerunds vs Infinitives.
  • Variation: Advanced B2 term related to Gerunds vs Infinitives.
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Executive Communication: High-level management uses these structures to project authority and clarity in strategic reports.

2. Academic Writing: Essential for university-level essays where you must analyze evidence and present balanced viewpoints.

3. Social Fluency: Allows you to engage in deep discussions about culture, politics, and abstract concepts with native-like ease.

3. Social Fluency: Allows you to engage in deep discussions about culture, politics, and abstract concepts with native-like ease.

title: Wish & If Only
theory:
🌟 Wish & If Only: Expressing Desires (B2)

We use 'wish' and 'if only' to talk about things we want to be different. There are three main patterns: Present Wish (Wish + Past Simple), Past Regret (Wish + Past Perfect), and Future/Behavior Desire (Wish + would). At the B2 level, these structures allow you to express a wide range of emotions from nostalgia to frustration.

🧩 The 3 Patterns
  • Present: I wish I were taller. (Imaginary now).
  • Past: I wish I had known. (Regret).
  • Annoyance: I wish he would stop talking. (Desire for change).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I wish I had more time. → ⏳ [Present wish].
  • 📝 If only I had studied harder! → 🎓 [Past regret].
  • 📝 I wish it would stop raining. → 🌧️ [Annoyance/Hope].
  • 📝 She wishes she could swim. → 🏊‍♀️ [Ability wish].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Lament (v): To express grief or regret. "To lament a lost chance."
  • Yearning (n): A feeling of intense longing. "A yearning for home."
  • Regret (n): Sadness about something done. "Living with regret."
  • Ideal (adj): Most suitable or perfect. "An ideal world."
  • Discontent (n): Dissatisfaction. "Expressing discontent."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Social Interactions: "I wish you could stay longer."

2. Personal Reflection: "If only I had taken that opportunity."

3. Complaining: "I wish the wifi would work properly!"

title: Perfect Modals
theory:
🕰️ Perfect Modals: Past Speculation (B2)

Perfect modals (Modal + have + V3) allow us to speculate, regret, or criticize past actions. At the B2 level, you must accurately choose between 'must have' (certainty), 'can't have' (impossibility), 'might have' (possibility), and 'should have' (advice not taken). This is the key to deep analytical thinking in English.

🧩 Logic Grid
  • Must have: I'm 90% sure it happened.
  • Can't have: I'm 90% sure it didn't.
  • Might/Could have: Maybe it happened.
  • Should have: It was a good idea (but failed).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 He must have forgotten the meeting. → 😴 [Logical deduction].
  • 📝 She can't have seen you; she was away. → ✈️ [Impossibility].
  • 📝 You should have told me! → 🗣️ [Past advice].
  • 📝 I might have left my phone at home. → 🏠 [Possibility].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Inference (n): A conclusion based on evidence. "Draw an inference."
  • Reconstruction (n): Rebuilding a past event. "A mental reconstruction."
  • Logic (n): Reasoning. "Flawless logic."
  • Explanation (n): A statement making something clear. "A plausible explanation."
  • Conclusion (n): The end or finish of a process. "Jump to a conclusion."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Investigating: "The thief must have entered through the roof."

2. Post-Meeting Analysis: "We should have discussed the budget first."

3. Misunderstandings: "She can't have meant what she said."

title: Subordinating Conjunctions
theory:
🔗 Connecting Ideas: Subordinating Conjunctions (B2)

Subordinating conjunctions (e.g., 'although', 'unless', 'while', 'because') link a dependent clause to an independent one, creating complex and sophisticated sentences. At the B2 level, we focus on nuances of contrast, condition, and time. Mastering these is the hallmark of an advanced writer who can build logical and fluid arguments.

🧩 Logic Groupings
  • Contrast: Although, even though, whereas, while.
  • Condition: Unless, provided that, as long as.
  • Time: As soon as, until, once, while.
  • Reason: Since, as, because.
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 Unless it rains, we'll have a BBQ. → 🍔 [Condition].
  • 📝 Although it was expensive, I bought it. → 🛍️ [Contrast].
  • 📝 Call me as soon as you arrive. → 📞 [Time].
  • 📝 Since we have time, let's talk. → 🗣️ [Reason].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Cohesion (n): The action of forming a united whole. "Improve cohesion."
  • Correlation (n): A mutual relationship. "A causal correlation."
  • Conditional (adj): Subject to one or more requirements. "A conditional offer."
  • Temporal (adj): Relating to time. "Temporal markers."
  • Causal (adj): Relating to a cause. "A causal link."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Academic Writing: "Although the data is limited, the results are clear."

2. Business Rules: "You can work from home as long as you finish the task."

3. Travel Plans: "I'll stay in the car while you go into the shop."

title: Present Perfect Continuous
theory:
⏳ Present Perfect Continuous: Ongoing Life (B2)

We use the Present Perfect Continuous (have been + -ing) to focus on the duration of an activity that started in the past and continues to the present, or has just finished and has a visible result. At the B2 level, we use it to express frustration, emphasize effort, or describe temporary habits.

🧩 The Formula

[Subject] + have/has been + [Verb + -ing]

  • Focus: The activity itself (not the result).
  • Time Markers: For, since, lately, recently, all day.
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I have been working all day. → 💻 [Continuous effort].
  • 📝 She has been studying for three hours. → 📚 [Duration].
  • 📝 It has been raining recently. → 🌧️ [Recent habit].
  • 📝 Why are you wet? I have been swimming. → 🏊‍♂️ [Result].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Continuous (adj): Without interruption. "A continuous process."
  • Duration (n): The time something continues. "The duration of the task."
  • Persistence (n): Continuing in a course of action. "Great persistence."
  • Accumulate (v): To gather or build up. "Accumulate experience."
  • Interval (n): A pause or break. "Regular intervals."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Work Updates: "We have been developing this software for two years."

2. Complaining: "I have been waiting for an hour!"

3. Small Talk: "What have you been doing lately?"

title: Used To / Be Used To / Get Used To
theory:
🔄 Used To vs Be/Get Used To: Adaptation (B2)

The biggest confusion in B2 is distinguishing between past habits ('Used to + verb') and the process of adaptation ('Be/Get used to + -ing'). One describes a finished past, while the others describe a present state of familiarity or a transition. Mastering these allows you to talk about cultural adaptation and life changes fluently.

🧩 The 3 Variants
  • Used to + Verb: Past habit (I used to smoke).
  • Be used to + -ing: Familiarity (I am used to the noise).
  • Get used to + -ing: Transition (I am getting used to it).
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 I used to live in London. → 🇬🇧 [Past habit].
  • 📝 I am used to driving on the left. → 🚗 [Familiarity].
  • 📝 I'm getting used to the heat. → ☀️ [Adaptation].
  • 📝 Did you use to swim? → 🏊‍♂️ [Question form].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Adaptation (n): Changing to suit conditions. "Cultural adaptation."
  • Familiarity (n): Close acquaintance. "A sense of familiarity."
  • Transition (n): Changing from one state to another. "A smooth transition."
  • Habitual (adj): Done by habit. "A habitual action."
  • Acclimate (v): Get used to a new climate or situation. "To acclimate quickly."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Moving Abroad: "It's hard at first, but you'll get used to the food."

2. Career Change: "I used to be an engineer, but now I'm a chef."

3. Commuting: "I am used to waking up at 5 AM."

title: Past Perfect Continuous
description: Ongoing actions before a past moment
theory:
✨ Past Perfect Continuous (B2 Mastery)

In the Upper-Intermediate level (B2), precision is paramount. This topic focuses on complex structures that allow you to express subtle shades of meaning, formal intentions, and sophisticated arguments. Mastering this will bridge the gap between being a functional speaker and a truly fluent communicator in professional and academic environments.

🎨 Visual Examples & Nuances
  • 📝 Specialized usage in a professional context. → 💼 [Professional Flair].
  • 📝 Complex sentence structure example. → 🧠 [Cognitive Depth].
  • 📝 Everyday advanced interaction. → 🤝 [Social Sophistication].
📚 Academic & Professional Vocabulary
  • Preceding: Advanced B2 term related to Past Perfect Continuous.
  • Duration: Advanced B2 term related to Past Perfect Continuous.
  • Background: Advanced B2 term related to Past Perfect Continuous.
  • Sequence: Advanced B2 term related to Past Perfect Continuous.
  • Interruption: Advanced B2 term related to Past Perfect Continuous.
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Executive Communication: High-level management uses these structures to project authority and clarity in strategic reports.

2. Academic Writing: Essential for university-level essays where you must analyze evidence and present balanced viewpoints.

3. Social Fluency: Allows you to engage in deep discussions about culture, politics, and abstract concepts with native-like ease.

title: Future Perfect Continuous
description: Duration up to a future point
theory:
✨ Future Perfect Continuous (B2 Mastery)

In the Upper-Intermediate level (B2), precision is paramount. This topic focuses on complex structures that allow you to express subtle shades of meaning, formal intentions, and sophisticated arguments. Mastering this will bridge the gap between being a functional speaker and a truly fluent communicator in professional and academic environments.

🎨 Visual Examples & Nuances
  • 📝 Specialized usage in a professional context. → 💼 [Professional Flair].
  • 📝 Complex sentence structure example. → 🧠 [Cognitive Depth].
  • 📝 Everyday advanced interaction. → 🤝 [Social Sophistication].
📚 Academic & Professional Vocabulary
  • Projection: Advanced B2 term related to Future Perfect Continuous.
  • Milestone: Advanced B2 term related to Future Perfect Continuous.
  • Anticipation: Advanced B2 term related to Future Perfect Continuous.
  • Longevity: Advanced B2 term related to Future Perfect Continuous.
  • Timeline: Advanced B2 term related to Future Perfect Continuous.
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Executive Communication: High-level management uses these structures to project authority and clarity in strategic reports.

2. Academic Writing: Essential for university-level essays where you must analyze evidence and present balanced viewpoints.

3. Social Fluency: Allows you to engage in deep discussions about culture, politics, and abstract concepts with native-like ease.

title: Mixed Conditionals
description: Combine past and present in conditionals
theory:
🌀 Mixed Conditionals: Past & Present (B2)

Mixed conditionals combine the past and the present in one sentence. They allow you to talk about a past action that has a present consequence (e.g., 'If I had won, I would be rich now') or a present state that affected a past event (e.g., 'If I were brave, I would have spoken'). This is the ultimate test of conditional mastery.

🧩 The Two Main Types
  • Past action → Present result: If I had taken the map, we wouldn't be lost.
  • Present state → Past result: If I were smarter, I would have known.
🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 If I hadn't missed my flight, I would be in Spain now. → 🇪🇸 [Past → Present].
  • 📝 If I spoke French, I would have understood. → 🇫🇷 [General → Past].
  • 📝 We would be famous if we had won. → 🌟 [Past → Present].
  • 📝 If she loved him, she would have said yes. → 💍 [General → Past].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Consequence (n): A result of an action. "The consequence is clear."
  • Hypothetical (adj): Based on an imaginary situation. "A hypothetical mix."
  • Integration (n): Combining things. "The integration of tenses."
  • Persistence (n): Continuing to exist. "The persistence of the past."
  • Scenario (n): A situation. "A complex scenario."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Regret Analysis: "If we had invested then, we would be wealthy now."

2. Logic Checks: "If I were a doctor, I would have helped him."

3. Debates: "If the economy were stronger, the war wouldn't have started."

title: Advanced Phrasal Verbs
description: Expert ESL content for C1 Mastery
theory:
🎓 Advanced Phrasal Verbs (C1)

At the C1 level, phrasal verbs are no longer just about movement or simple actions. We explore metaphorical meanings and the subtle nuances that particles (up, down, off, out) add to a verb. Mastering these allows for a more natural, idiomatic flow in both academic writing and professional discourse, enabling you to express complex concepts with the precision of a native speaker.

🧩 Grammar Formula

[Verb] + [Particle] + [Metaphorical Context]

Example: "To (iron) (out) a (discrepancy)."

🎨 Visual Examples
  • 📝 We need to iron out the final details of the contract. → 📄 [Resolving minor issues].
  • 📝 The CEO glossed over the financial losses during the meeting. → 🙈 [Avoiding a difficult topic].
  • 📝 I finally cottoned on to what they were actually planning. → 💡 [Realizing or understanding].
  • 📝 The news of the merger brought about a lot of anxiety. → 🌀 [Causing a change].
📚 Academic Vocabulary Focus
  • Nuance (n): A subtle difference in meaning. "Understanding the nuances."
  • Discourse (n): Written or spoken communication. "Professional discourse."
  • Metaphorical (adj): Figurative, not literal. "A metaphorical usage."
  • Idiomatic (adj): Natural to a native speaker. "Using idiomatic English."
  • Discrepancy (n): A lack of compatibility. "Resolving a discrepancy."
🌎 English in the Real World

1. Corporate Negotiations: "We must hammer out an agreement before the deadline."

2. Academic Analysis: "The study points out several inconsistencies in the data."

3. Crisis Management: "The PR team tried to play down the impact of the scandal."
